title: ViT-K: A Few-Shot Learning Model for Coupled Fluid-Porous Media Flows with Interface Conditions
summary: The numerical simulation of interaction between free flow and porous media, governed by coupled Stokes/Navier--Stokes--Darcy flows, is critical for understanding fluid filtration and physiological transport, yet it is hindered by the high computational cost of resolving interface heterogeneities and the instability of long-term predictions. While deep learning offers surrogate modeling potential, existing frameworks often suffer from exponential error accumulation and poor convergence in multi-physics regimes. To address these limitations, we propose ViT-K, a novel few-shot learning model designed to learn the spatiotemporal evolution of coupled flows from sparse datasets. The ViT-K framework effectively reconstructs the global flow physics on a low-dimensional manifold by combining Vision Transformers (ViT) to capture heterogeneous interfacial features with the Koopman operator to linearize temporal dynamics. By lifting nonlinear dynamics into a globally linear observable space, the ViT-K model provides stability by design, ensuring that prediction errors grow linearly rather than exponentially over time. This theoretical property enables reliable long-term extrapolation even in small-sample regimes. Numerical experiments on benchmark coupled systems demonstrate that ViT-K not only captures complex interface physics with high fidelity but also exhibits exceptional robustness against measurement noise by acting as an implicit spectral filter. The proposed method significantly outperforms traditional solvers in inference speed while maintaining physical consistency, offering a robust paradigm for real-time multiphysics forecasting.

AI.tldr: ViT-K is a few-shot learning model combining Vision Transformers and Koopman operator for stable, fast, and accurate long-term prediction of coupled Stokes/Navier-Stokes-Darcy flows from sparse data.
AI.motivation: High computational cost and instability in long-term predictions of coupled free flow and porous media (Stokes/Navier-Stokes-Darcy) simulations; existing deep learning surrogates suffer from exponential error accumulation and poor convergence.
AI.method: ViT-K combines Vision Transformers (ViT) to capture heterogeneous interfacial features with the Koopman operator to linearize temporal dynamics, lifting nonlinear dynamics into a globally linear observable space for stable long-term extrapolation.
AI.result: ViT-K captures complex interface physics with high fidelity, exhibits exceptional robustness against measurement noise (acting as an implicit spectral filter), achieves linear error growth over time, and significantly outperforms traditional solvers in inference speed while maintaining physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: ViT-K offers a robust, stable, and efficient paradigm for real-time multiphysics forecasting in coupled flow systems, particularly valuable in small-sample regimes.

title: $\ell$FEM: An efficient loop-free Matlab implementation of isoparametric bulk and surface finite elements
summary:
The $\ell$FEM MATLAB package provides a simple, efficient, and flexible implementation of isoparametric finite elements in bulk domains and on surfaces. The finite element matrix assemblies are based on MATLAB's paged operators and therefore completely loop-free. We give a short and conscious description of high-order isoparametric surface finite elements, which is then used to describe the assembly process and the implementation.
  We report on relevant numerical experiments (runtime comparisons, modifications for non-linear problems, etc.), and on additional functions, examples, and a testing unit which are all part of the $\ell$FEM package.

AI.tldr: The ℓFEM MATLAB package implements loop-free, high-order isoparametric finite elements for bulk and surface domains using MATLAB's paged operators, with a focus on simplicity, efficiency, and flexibility.
AI.motivation: To provide a simple, efficient, and flexible loop-free implementation of isoparametric finite elements on both bulk domains and surfaces using MATLAB's paged operators.
AI.method: The package uses MATLAB's paged operators to achieve completely loop-free matrix assemblies for isoparametric finite elements, with a conscious description of high-order isoparametric surface finite elements guiding the implementation.
AI.result: Runtime comparisons, modifications for non-linear problems, and additional functions, examples, and a testing unit are reported, demonstrating the package's capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The ℓFEM package offers a loop-free, efficient implementation of finite elements suitable for both bulk and surface problems, with demonstrated performance and extensibility.

title: Goal-Oriented Time Adaptivity for Linear Port-Hamiltonian Differential-Algebraic Equations of Index~1
summary: Port-Hamiltonian systems provide a highly-structured framework for modeling of physical systems. By definition, they encode a balance equation relating energy changes to supplied and dissipated energy. Capturing this energy balance in discrete approximations is a fundamental challenge and often has been achieved by designing particular schemes such as discrete gradient methods. In this work, we propose an approach that controls the energy balance violation for port-Hamiltonian differential algebraic equations via time adaptivity using a posteriori grid refinement techniques based on the dual weighted residual method. In particular, we show how one may leverage the port-Hamiltonian structure to efficiently compute the error estimators using a dissipativity-exploiting block-Jacobi approximation. We illustrate the efficacy of the method by means of simulations of electrical circuit models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a time-adaptive method for port-Hamiltonian differential algebraic equations that controls energy balance violations using dual weighted residual-based grid refinement, with an efficient block-Jacobi error estimator exploiting dissipativity.
AI.motivation: Capturing the energy balance in discrete approximations of port-Hamiltonian systems is a fundamental challenge, and existing approaches like discrete gradient methods are scheme-specific.
AI.method: The authors control energy balance violation via time adaptivity using a posteriori grid refinement based on the dual weighted residual method, leveraging port-Hamiltonian structure to compute error estimators efficiently via a dissipativity-exploiting block-Jacobi approximation.
AI.result: The method's efficacy is illustrated through simulations of electrical circuit models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides an efficient, structure-preserving way to enforce energy balance in numerical approximations of port-Hamiltonian systems without relying on specific discretization schemes.

title: Classification of Double Saddle-Point Systems
summary: We offer a classification of a broad and practically relevant class of symmetric double saddle-point system. At the core of the paper is the division of the associated matrices into ``block-arrow'' and ``block-tridiagonal'' forms. We describe relevant applications, invertibility conditions, spectral properties, and block preconditioners. Our discussion is kept within a general framework rather than tailored to specific applications.

AI.tldr: This paper classifies symmetric double saddle-point systems into block-arrow and block-tridiagonal forms, covering applications, invertibility conditions, spectral properties, and block preconditioners within a general framework.
AI.motivation: To provide a broad classification of symmetric double saddle-point systems, unifying theory across diverse applications rather than focusing on specific cases.
AI.method: The authors classify the matrices into two forms: "block-arrow" and "block-tridiagonal," then analyze invertibility conditions, spectral properties, and block preconditioners within this general framework.
AI.result: A systematic classification of double saddle-point matrices with associated theoretical analysis and preconditioning strategies.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a general, application-agnostic framework for understanding and preconditioning symmetric double saddle-point systems.

title: Boris and Exponential Integrators in the Theory of Particles Interacting with Magnetic Turbulence
summary: The interaction of electrically charged particles with magnetic fields is a fundamental problem in several areas of physics. An example is the motion of energetic particles through a magnetized plasma. The most accurate and reliable way to explore theoretically the interactions between particles and fields is via test-particle simulations. In such simulations one creates the turbulent magnetic field and solves the Newton-Lorentz equation numerically by employing an integration scheme. In the current article we discuss exponential integrators and derive systematically from this the Rodrigues scheme as well as the famous Boris integrator. For an approach where one creates the magnetic field anew at each time step, both integrators are overall comparable. In theory the Rodrigues approach should be more accurate due to the fact that the occurring matrix exponential is evaluated without further approximations. Practically, both methods provide very similar results. It is argued in the current article that a Rodrigues based integrator is a very strong alternative because for the specific problem discussed here, it does not require longer computing times.

AI.tldr: This paper compares exponential integrators (Rodrigues scheme) with the Boris integrator for test-particle simulations in magnetized plasmas, finding both methods produce similar results but the Rodrigues approach offers a competitive alternative with comparable computing times.
AI.motivation: The motion of charged particles in magnetic fields is fundamental across physics, and test-particle simulations are the most reliable theoretical tool. The authors aim to systematically derive and compare integration schemes (Rodrigues and Boris) for this problem.
AI.method: The authors systematically derive exponential integrators from a theoretical foundation, obtaining the Rodrigues scheme and the Boris integrator. They compare both methods in test-particle simulations where the turbulent magnetic field is regenerated at each time step.
AI.result: Both integrators produce very similar results in practice. Theoretically, the Rodrigues approach should be more accurate because it evaluates the matrix exponential without approximations, but practically the difference is negligible. The Rodrigues method does not require longer computing times for this specific problem.
AI.conclusion: The Rodrigues-based integrator is a strong alternative to the Boris integrator for charged particle simulations in magnetic fields, offering comparable accuracy and computational cost.

title: Energy identity for stationary biharmonic mappings into spheres in supercritical dimensions
summary: Energy identity for harmonic type maps in supercritical dimensions is an important and difficult problem. For sphere-valued harmonic maps, the first breakthrough was achieved by Lin-Rivière [Duke Math. J. 2002]. In this paper, by adapting their strategy, we establish the energy identity for stationary biharmonic maps into spheres in supercritical dimensions $n\ge 5$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves an energy identity for stationary biharmonic maps into spheres in dimensions n≥5, adapting the strategy of Lin-Rivière.
AI.motivation: The energy identity for harmonic-type maps in supercritical dimensions is a known difficult problem, and this paper extends prior results (Lin-Rivière for harmonic maps) to the biharmonic case.
AI.method: The authors adapt Lin-Rivière's strategy from the harmonic map setting to the biharmonic map setting.
AI.result: Energy identity is established for stationary biharmonic maps into spheres when n≥5.
AI.conclusion: The result generalizes a known energy identity from harmonic maps to biharmonic maps in supercritical dimensions.

title: Fast contracted Clebsch--Gordan tensor products for equivariant graph neural networks
summary: We present an $\mathcal{O}(L^3)$ algorithm for evaluating contracted Clebsch--Gordan tensor products in $\mathrm{O}(3)$-equivariant machine learning potentials at fixed Canonical Polyadic (CP) rank. Mapping the angular integral to a structured Gauss--Legendre and Fourier tensor-product grid decouples the radial channel contractions from the angular transforms. The antisymmetric parity-odd Clebsch--Gordan channels, unreachable by the symmetric pointwise product on a scalar $S^2$ grid, are recovered through the surface-curl pairing $\hat r \cdot [\nabla_{S^2} A \times \nabla_{S^2} B]$, the spherical Poisson bracket, which supplies the $L=1$ angular momentum on the grid while preserving rotational equivariance. The construction extends to parity-aware equivariant message passing in atomic-cluster-expansion-style architectures and is verified by direct numerical quadrature. The full uncontracted Clebsch--Gordan tensor product remains subject to the $\mathcal{O}(L^4)$ output-size lower bound. A benchmark shows wall-clock scaling empirically as $L^2$ across the practical $l_{\max}$ range. For the on-site contraction this is pre-asymptotic, giving way to $L^3$ at large $l_{\max}$. For message passing it is structural and the runtime is memory-bandwidth bound on $L^2$-sized grid tensors.

AI.tldr: Proposes an O(L³) algorithm for evaluating Clebsch-Gordan tensor products in O(3)-equivariant ML potentials using structured grids and spherical Poisson brackets, with empirical L² scaling observed in practical ranges.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of contracted Clebsch-Gordan tensor products in equivariant machine learning potentials, which are typically expensive due to angular integration, especially at higher angular momentum cutoffs.
AI.method: Maps angular integrals to a Gauss-Legendre and Fourier tensor-product grid; decouples radial contractions from angular transforms; uses surface-curl pairing (spherical Poisson bracket) to recover parity-odd channels that symmetric pointwise products on S² grids cannot capture.
AI.result: Achieves O(L³) algorithmic scaling (vs. O(L⁴) lower bound for uncontracted products); empirical wall-clock scaling observed as L² in practical l_max range; on-site contraction is pre-asymptotic (giving way to L³ at large l_max); message passing is memory-bandwidth bound on L²-sized grid tensors.
AI.conclusion: The grid-based approach with spherical Poisson bracket enables efficient parity-aware equivariant message passing in ACE-style architectures, verified by numerical quadrature.

title: Wavelet-Based Observables for Koopman Analysis: An Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition Framework
summary: We present an in-depth analysis of the Koopman semigroup via wavelet transform. Towards this goal, we start by introducing the wavelet-based observables and show that they are eigenfunctions of the Koopman semigroup when this semigroup is considered over the Banach space of continuous functions on a compact forward-invariant set endowed with the supremum norm. We then construct closed-form expressions of the action of the Koopman semigroup and its resolvent in terms of these observables. To approximate the action of Koopman semigroup numerically, we combine Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) with the proposed wavelet-based observables leading to the Wavelet Dynamic Mode Decomposition via Continuous Wavelet Transform (cWDMD) algorithm. We validate our theoretical results on two numerical examples.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces wavelet-based observables as eigenfunctions of the Koopman semigroup and develops the cWDMD algorithm combining EDMD with wavelet transforms for numerical approximation.
AI.motivation: To provide a deeper theoretical analysis of the Koopman semigroup using wavelet transforms and to develop a more effective numerical method for approximating its action.
AI.method: Introduces wavelet-based observables as eigenfunctions of the Koopman semigroup over the Banach space of continuous functions on a compact forward-invariant set. Derives closed-form expressions for the Koopman semigroup and its resolvent. Proposes the cWDMD (Wavelet Dynamic Mode Decomposition via Continuous Wavelet Transform) algorithm combining EDMD with wavelet-based observables.
AI.result: Theoretical results are validated on two numerical examples demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed wavelet-based approach and cWDMD algorithm.
AI.conclusion: Wavelet-based observables provide a new theoretical foundation for Koopman operator analysis, and the cWDMD algorithm offers a promising numerical framework for approximating Koopman semigroup dynamics.

title: A Hybrid Scheme to Achieve Highest Implosion Performance on the OMEGA Laser
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables, submitted to Physical Review Letters; author-prepared manuscript
summary: Merging direct and indirect-drive has long been viewed as an optimal hybrid laser-fusion scheme that combines the uniformity of x rays with the efficiency of direct illumination. We present the first integrated 2D simulations of hybrid shock drive (HSD) targets for the OMEGA laser. The HSD scheme [L. Ceurvorst et al., Phys. Rev. E 101 063207 (2020)] uses x rays from a thin Au-coated x-ray converter outer shell to drive the initial shock into a standard direct-drive capsule. Direct illumination is used to implode the target after the first shock. The design effectively suppresses laser-imprint seeding of hydrodynamic instabilities, maintaining shell integrity during the implosion. This scheme will enable fielding low-adiabat, high-convergence implosions on OMEGA with expected performance greatly exceeding those of current designs. HSD targets are projected to significantly enhance fusion yields, potentially increasing the record Lawson parameter by $\sim$85\% on OMEGA while effectively eliminating the requirement for laser smoothing. These results position HSD as a robust platform for high-performance implosions, paving the way for advanced high-gain inertial fusion energy targets.

AI.tldr: First integrated 2D simulations of hybrid shock drive (HSD) targets on OMEGA show this scheme combines x-ray pre-shock (from Au-coated shell) with direct-drive implosion, suppressing laser-imprint instabilities and potentially increasing the record Lawson parameter by ~85% while eliminating need for laser smoothing.
AI.motivation: Merging direct and indirect-drive has been viewed as an optimal hybrid laser-fusion scheme combining x-ray uniformity with direct illumination efficiency, but had not been demonstrated in integrated simulations.
AI.method: Integrated 2D simulations of HSD targets on OMEGA laser; uses thin Au-coated x-ray converter outer shell to drive initial shock via x rays, then direct illumination implodes the target after first shock.
AI.result: HSD effectively suppresses laser-imprint seeding of hydrodynamic instabilities, maintains shell integrity, enables low-adiabat high-convergence implosions, projected to increase record Lawson parameter by ~85% on OMEGA while eliminating laser smoothing requirements.
AI.conclusion: HSD is a robust platform for high-performance implosions, paving way for advanced high-gain inertial fusion energy targets.

title: The "good" Boussinesq equation on the half-line with Robin boundary conditions
summary: We prove the local Hadamard well-posedness of the ``good'' Boussinesq equation formulated on the half-line with nonzero Robin boundary conditions. These boundary data involve the Dirichlet and Neumann boundary values as well as the second spatial derivative of the solution evaluated at the boundary. The nonlinear analysis crucially relies on the linear estimates established through the explicit solution formula obtained for the forced linear counterpart of the problem via Fokas's unified transform. The two pieces of initial data and the two pieces of boundary data belong in appropriate Sobolev spaces. The corresponding solution is established in the natural Hadamard solution space of continuous/continuously differentiable functions from a suitable time interval to the Sobolev spaces associated with the two initial data. Furthermore, in line with the well-posedness theory of the Cauchy problem, in the case of low regularity (namely, below the spatial continuity threshold) the solution space is refined by also including an appropriate spatiotemporal Lebesgue space.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes local Hadamard well-posedness for the "good" Boussinesq equation on a half-line with nonzero Robin boundary conditions, using Fokas's unified transform to derive linear estimates, and treating both regular and low-regularity regimes.
AI.motivation: To extend the well-posedness theory of the "good" Boussinesq equation from the Cauchy (whole-line) problem to the half-line setting with physically relevant nonzero Robin boundary conditions, which involve a combination of Dirichlet, Neumann, and second-derivative boundary values.
AI.method: The analysis relies on linear estimates derived from an explicit solution formula for the forced linear problem via Fokas's unified transform. The nonlinear problem is then treated using these estimates, with initial and boundary data placed in appropriate Sobolev spaces. For low-regularity data (below the spatial continuity threshold), the solution space is refined to include spatiotemporal Lebesgue spaces.
AI.result: The paper proves local Hadamard well-posedness: existence, uniqueness, and continuous dependence on data. The solution resides in a space of continuous/continuously differentiable functions mapping a time interval to Sobolev spaces associated with the initial data. In the low-regularity case, an additional spatiotemporal Lebesgue component is incorporated.
AI.conclusion: The half-line problem for the "good" Boussinesq equation with nonzero Robin boundary conditions is locally well-posed in the Hadamard sense, analogous to the Cauchy problem, with appropriate refinements for low-regularity regimes.

title: Unified definition of ferroelectricity
summary: Recent theoretical and experimental advances in quantum ferroelectrics suggest that ferroelectricity can also emerge in non-polar space group, highlighting the limitations of conventional polar space group criteria in identifying ferroelectric materials. Here, we introduce a unified definition based on switchable polarization differences between energetically equivalent states, which naturally encompasses conventional and quantum ferroelectrics. Guided by this principle, we implement a high-throughput screening strategy that systematically identifies both conventional and quantum ferroelectrics among experimentally synthesized materials. In particular, we identify a new type of quantum ferroelectric in which the quantized polarization arises from arbitrary ionic displacements, in contrast to previous quantum ferroelectrics (including both fractional and integer quantum ferroelectrics) where quantized polarization results from fractional or integer ionic displacements. Notably, we find that materials such as Ba3I6 and Cs2PdC2 exhibit low switching barriers and robust insulating behavior, highlighting their experimental viability. Our results reconcile conventional and quantum ferroelectrics, expand the accessible materials landscape, and provide a practical roadmap for discovering next-generation ferroelectrics with advanced switchable functionalities.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified definition for ferroelectrics based on switchable polarization between equivalent states, enabling high-throughput screening that identifies both conventional and quantum ferroelectrics, including a new type where quantized polarization arises from arbitrary ionic displacements.
AI.motivation: Recent advances show ferroelectricity can emerge in non-polar space groups, challenging traditional polar-space-group criteria. A unified framework is needed to capture both conventional and quantum ferroelectrics.
AI.method: The authors introduce a unified definition based on switchable polarization differences between energetically equivalent states, then implement a high-throughput screening strategy over experimentally synthesized materials.
AI.result: Identified new quantum ferroelectrics where quantized polarization arises from arbitrary (rather than fractional/integer) ionic displacements. Ba3I6 and Cs2PdC2 show low switching barriers and robust insulating behavior.
AI.conclusion: The work reconciles conventional and quantum ferroelectrics, expands the materials landscape, and provides a practical roadmap for discovering next-generation ferroelectrics.

title: Neural Networks for Singular Perturbations -- Finite Regularity
summary: We study finite-element and deep feedforward neural network (DNN for short) expressivity rate bounds for solution sets of a model linear, second order singularly perturbed, elliptic two-point boundary value problem, in Sobolev norms on a bounded interval $(-1,1)$, with explicit dependence on the singular perturbation parameter $\e\in (0,1]$. Emphasis is on low Sobolev regularity of the data, i.e., source term $f$ and reaction coefficient $b$. A proof of $\e$-explicit solution regularity based on exponentially weighted energy-norm bounds is developed, and \emph{$\e$-robust, algebraic expression rate bounds} in Sobolev norms for $\mathbb{P}_1$ Finite-Elements on exponential and Shishkin type meshes is proved. Expression rates for shallow (fixed depth) $\ReLU$-NNs are shown which are robust w.r. to $\e$ and explicit in terms of the NN size. Robust NN expression rate bounds are further studied for deep feedforward DNNs with ReLU and tanh-activations. As in \cite{OSX24_1085}, tanh- and sigmoid-activated sub-NNs allow to include exponential boundary layer functions exactly into the NN feature space, leading to reduced NN sizes. Recent bitstring encoding techniques for deep NNs with ReLU activations afford, still under low data regularity $f,b \in H^1(I)$ \emph{twice the (robust) convergence rate of $\mathbb{P}_1$ Finite-Elements} achievable with ``eXp'' or Shishkin meshes.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes expressivity rates of finite elements and deep neural networks for solving singularly perturbed elliptic boundary value problems, showing that ReLU-activated deep NNs achieve twice the robust convergence rate of P1 finite elements on specialized meshes, even under low data regularity.
AI.motivation: To derive ε-robust algebraic expression rate bounds for both finite-element and neural network approximations of singularly perturbed problems with low Sobolev regularity data, explicitly quantifying dependence on the singular perturbation parameter ε.
AI.method: Develops exponentially weighted energy-norm bounds for ε-explicit solution regularity; proves ε-robust rate bounds for P1 finite elements on exponential/Shishkin meshes; analyzes shallow ReLU NNs and deep feedforward NNs with ReLU and tanh activations; uses tanh/sigmoid sub-NNs to embed exponential boundary layers and bitstring encoding for deep ReLU NNs.
AI.result: Deep ReLU NNs achieve twice the robust convergence rate of P1 finite elements on specialized meshes under low data regularity (f,b ∈ H¹(I)); tanh/sigmoid subnetworks incorporate boundary layers exactly, reducing NN size; all rates are ε-robust.
AI.conclusion: Deep neural networks, particularly with ReLU activations and bitstring encoding, offer superior ε-robust approximation rates for singularly perturbed problems compared to classical finite elements, even when data regularity is low.

title: Few-Attosecond electron pulse trains with tunable periods produced by two counter-propagating lasers
summary: Attosecond electron pulses enable real-time probing of ultrafast matter dynamics, yet conventional modulation schemes suffer from drastically shortened longitudinal focal lengths when targeting sub-attosecond durations. To address this bottleneck, we propose and demonstrate a compact scheme utilizing two counter-propagating lasers that reveals a previously unidentified stable modulation regime. Contrary to established models of ponderomotive forces and stochastic acceleration in dual-laser fields, we show that a specific parametric resonance condition permits the electron beam to be stably modulated into highly periodic attosecond trains with rapid energy gain. Using a sub-relativistic electron beam, simulations confirm the generation of ~1 as pulses with a Lorentz factor up to 15 and a relative energy spread below 0.02%, extending the focal length by three orders of magnitude compared with conventional approaches. This work identifies the critical transition from ordered modulation to stochastic acceleration, offering a viable route to overcoming the focal-length barrier in attosecond electron-pulse applications.

AI.tldr: A compact dual-laser scheme achieves stable modulation of electron beams into ~1 attosecond pulses with extended focal length, overcoming the focal-length bottleneck in attosecond electron-pulse generation.
AI.motivation: Conventional attosecond electron pulse modulation schemes suffer from drastically shortened longitudinal focal lengths when targeting sub-attosecond durations, limiting practical applications.
AI.method: Two counter-propagating lasers under a specific parametric resonance condition enable stable modulation of a sub-relativistic electron beam into highly periodic attosecond trains with rapid energy gain.
AI.result: Simulations demonstrate generation of ~1 as pulses with Lorentz factor up to 15, relative energy spread below 0.02%, and focal length extended by three orders of magnitude over conventional approaches.
AI.conclusion: This work identifies the critical transition from ordered modulation to stochastic acceleration, offering a viable route to overcoming the focal-length barrier in attosecond electron-pulse applications.

title: Well-posedness theorems in fluid-structure interaction: perfectly elastic shells
summary: In this work, we consider the interaction of a 3D incompressible fluid with a 2D flexible shell that occupies (a part of) the boundary of the fluid domain. We assume that the shell is perfectly elastic while the fluid is governed by the Navier--Stokes equations. Consequently, damping within the coupled system comes entirely from the parabolic fluid subsystem. Our main result is the construction of a local-in-time unique strong solution to the system of PDEs. Standard techniques from the literature do not apply here. They are restricted to visco-elastic structures, where the corresponding solid phase is parabolic. Our construction relies on a different method built upon a new estimate for the acceleration of the system. In the case of a 2D viscous incompressible fluid interacting with a 1D perfectly elastic shell we can extend the local solution globally in time (until a possible self-intersection of the shell).

AI.tldr: The paper proves existence of local-in-time unique strong solutions for a 3D Navier-Stokes fluid interacting with a 2D perfectly elastic shell (no structural damping), using a novel acceleration estimate. For a 2D fluid with a 1D elastic shell, the solution is extended globally until shell self-intersection.
AI.motivation: To solve the fluid-structure interaction problem where the shell is perfectly elastic (undamped), making standard parabolic-based techniques inapplicable, requiring a new method.
AI.method: The authors develop a new estimate for the acceleration of the system, enabling the construction of a local-in-time strong solution without relying on structural damping (viscoelasticity).
AI.result: Local-in-time unique strong solution for 3D fluid + 2D elastic shell; global-in-time solution for the 2D fluid + 1D elastic shell case (until possible self-intersection).
AI.conclusion: The work overcomes the limitation that previous methods required viscoelastic (parabolic) structures, extending existence results to perfectly elastic shells.

title: Deciphering Neural Reparameterized Full-Waveform Inversion with Neural Sensitivity Kernel and Wave Tangent Kernel
summary: Full-waveform inversion (FWI) estimates unknown parameters in the wave equation from limited boundary measurements. Recent advances in neural reparameterized FWI (NeurFWI) demonstrate that representing the parameters using a neural network can reduce the reliance on the high-quality initial model and wavefield data, at the cost of slow high-resolution convergence. However, its underlying theoretical mechanism remains unclear. In this study, we establish the neural sensitivity kernel (NSK) and the wave tangent kernel (WTK) to analyze their convergence behavior from both model and data domains. These theoretical frameworks show that the neural tangent kernel (NTK) induced by neural representation adaptively modulates the original sensitivity and wave tangent kernels. This modulation leads to several key outcomes, i.e., the spectral filtering effect, the gradient wavenumber modulation, and the wave frequency bias, connecting the convergence behavior of NeurFWI with the eigen-structures of NSK and WTK. Building on these insights, we propose several enhanced NeurFWI methods with tailored eigen-structures in NSK and WTK to improve inversion performances and efficiency. We numerically validate these theoretical claims and the proposed methods in seismic exploration, and firstly extend their application to medical imaging.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes neural sensitivity kernel (NSK) and wave tangent kernel (WTK) frameworks to explain why neural reparameterized FWI converges slowly at high resolutions, then develops enhanced methods with tailored eigen-structures to improve performance in seismic exploration and medical imaging.
AI.motivation: Neural reparameterized FWI (NeurFWI) reduces reliance on high-quality initial models and wavefield data, but its slow high-resolution convergence lacks theoretical explanation. This work aims to uncover the underlying mechanism.
AI.method: Establishes NSK and WTK theoretical frameworks to analyze convergence from model and data domains. Shows NTK adaptively modulates sensitivity/wave tangent kernels, producing spectral filtering, gradient wavenumber modulation, and wave frequency bias. Proposes enhanced NeurFWI methods with tailored eigen-structures.
AI.result: Demonstrates that NTK-induced modulation causes spectral filtering, wavenumber modulation, and frequency bias in NeurFWI convergence. Numerical validation in seismic exploration and first-time extension to medical imaging confirm improved performance.
AI.conclusion: The NSK/WTK frameworks successfully explain NeurFWI's convergence behavior, and the proposed eigen-structure-tailored methods enhance inversion efficiency and accuracy across both geophysical and medical applications.

title: Verification of reciprocity in anisotropic poroelastic wave simulation using symmetric Strang splitting
comment: SEG Expanded abstract, peer-reviewed and accepted
summary:
Poroelastic wave simulations are important for many applications relating fluid flow and wave characteristics in porous rock formations. Reciprocity is a key physical property of wave propagation in porous media that is important for such applications, even when viscous dissipation is present. However, numerical poroelastic simulations often fail to reproduce reciprocal responses because the discretization does not preserve the balance between reversible wave dynamics and irreversible fluid-solid drag. To address this, we formulate the Biot equations in terms of a continuous evolution operator split into a reversible (skew-adjoint) wave part and an irreversible (self-adjoint, non-positive) Darcy part, including the leading-order Johnson-Koplik-Dashen correction. This structure clarifies why reciprocity holds in the continuous equations and how it is easily broken in discrete form.
  Guided by this interpretation, we construct a symmetric second-order Strang-splitting scheme with half-step source injection. The method conserves energy in the reversible subsystem, treats Darcy dissipation unconditionally stably, and retains Courant limits similar to elastic solvers. Using a staggered pseudo-spectral discretization, we model multimode propagation in 2D VTI media and obtain cross-component reciprocity with a relative L2 misfit approaching machine precision, demonstrating that the discrete scheme inherits the symmetry properties of the continuous evolution operator.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Strang-splitting numerical scheme for poroelastic wave simulations that preserves reciprocity by separating the Biot equations into reversible (skew-adjoint) and irreversible (self-adjoint) parts, achieving machine-precision cross-component reciprocity in 2D VTI media.
AI.motivation: Poroelastic wave simulations often fail to reproduce reciprocal responses because standard discretization methods do not preserve the balance between reversible wave dynamics and irreversible fluid-solid drag, limiting their reliability for applications involving fluid flow and wave characteristics in porous rock formations.
AI.method: The authors formulate Biot equations as a continuous evolution operator split into a reversible skew-adjoint wave part and an irreversible self-adjoint Darcy part (including Johnson-Koplik-Dashen correction), then construct a symmetric second-order Strang-splitting scheme with half-step source injection using staggered pseudo-spectral discretization.
AI.result: The method conserves energy in the reversible subsystem, treats Darcy dissipation unconditionally stably, retains Courant limits similar to elastic solvers, and achieves cross-component reciprocity with a relative L2 misfit approaching machine precision in 2D VTI media.
AI.conclusion: The discrete scheme inherits the symmetry properties of the continuous evolution operator, demonstrating that proper operator splitting preserves reciprocity in numerical poroelastic simulations.

title: Real-time virtual circuits for plasma shape control via neural network emulators
summary: Reliable position and shape control in tokamak plasmas requires accurate real-time regulation of several strongly coupled shape parameters. The control vectors that disentangle these couplings, referred to as \textit{virtual circuits} (VCs), enable independent shape parameter control for a specific Grad--Shafranov (GS) equilibrium. Numerical calculation of VCs is not currently feasible in real time, therefore VCs are usually computed prior to each experiment, using a small number of reference GS equilibria sampled along the desired scenario trajectory, with each VC used to control the plasma within a preset time interval. While effective near the reference equilibrium, this approach can lead to degraded performance as the plasma departs from the reference equilibrium and/or from the desired trajectory, and it complicates the design of robust control strategies for rapidly evolving plasma configurations. In this paper, we construct neural-network-based emulators of plasma shape parameters from which VCs can be derived, to provide the MAST Upgrade (MAST-U) plasma control system with state-aware VCs in real-time. To do this, we develop an extensive library of over a million simulated GS equilibria, covering a substantial portion of the MAST-U operational space. These emulators provide differentiable functions whose gradients can be rapidly computed, enabling the derivation of accurate VCs for real-time shape control. We perform extensive verification of the emulated VCs by testing whether they disentangle the control problem. The neural-network-based approach delivers high accuracy and orthogonality across a diverse range of equilibria. This work establishes the physical validity of emulated VCs as a scalable and general alternative to schedules of precomputed VCs.

AI.tldr: This paper develops neural-network-based emulators to compute virtual circuits (VCs) in real-time for plasma shape control in tokamaks, replacing precomputed schedules and improving performance across diverse plasma equilibria in MAST Upgrade.
AI.motivation: Traditional precomputed virtual circuits degrade performance when plasma departs from reference equilibria, and robust control strategies are complicated for rapidly evolving plasma configurations. Real-time computation of VCs is not feasible with numerical methods.
AI.method: A library of over a million simulated Grad-Shafranov equilibria covering MAST-U operational space was built. Neural-network-based emulators provide differentiable functions for rapid gradient computation, enabling derivation of accurate VCs in real-time.
AI.result: The neural-network approach delivers high accuracy and orthogonality across a diverse range of equilibria, verified by testing whether emulated VCs successfully disentangle the control problem.
AI.conclusion: Emulated VCs are established as a scalable and general alternative to scheduled precomputed VCs, with proven physical validity for real-time plasma shape control.

title: Local and global solutions to continuous fragmentation-coagulation equations with vanishing diffusion and unbounded fragmentation and coagulation rates
summary: In the paper, we study spatially distributed particle systems whose time evolution is governed by vanishing diffusion in space $\mathbb{R}^d$, $d\ge 1$, and by size-continuous fragmentation and coagulation processes with unbounded rates. We show that for a large class of coefficients, such systems are classically locally well-posed, provided the diffusion and the coagulation processes are suitably dominated by the fragmentation. In the special case of power rates, we demonstrate existence of global in time classical solutions in all spatial dimensions $d\ge 1$ and without any restrictions on the size of input data.

AI.tldr: This paper studies spatially distributed particle systems with vanishing diffusion, fragmentation, and coagulation. It proves local well-posedness when fragmentation dominates diffusion and coagulation, and global existence for power rates in any dimension without input data restrictions.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of particle systems in space where diffusion is very weak but fragmentation and coagulation occur at high rates, and to establish rigorous mathematical foundations for such systems.
AI.method: The authors analyze spatially distributed particle systems with vanishing diffusion in ℝ^d, combined with size-continuous fragmentation and coagulation processes having unbounded rates. They impose conditions where fragmentation suitably dominates diffusion and coagulation.
AI.result: Classical local well-posedness is established for a large class of coefficients under dominance conditions. For power-law rates, global in time classical solutions exist in all dimensions d ≥ 1 with no restrictions on input data size.
AI.conclusion: Fragmentation-dominated regimes allow well-posedness of these complex particle systems, and power-rate cases are particularly robust, yielding global solutions universally.

title: Three dimensional simulation of fluid-driven frictional and tensile ruptures on existing discontinuities
summary: We present an implicit, fully-coupled hydro-mechanical solver for the three dimensional simulation of fluid-driven rupture propagation along existing discontinuities. The solver handles simultaneously frictional slip (shear failure) and tensile opening (hydraulic fracture) along arbitrary intersecting fractures and faults in a linearly elastic and impermeable rock matrix. The spatial discretization combines a collocation displacement discontinuity boundary element method for quasi-static elasticity with a Galerkin finite element method for nonlinear pore-fluid diffusion along the discontinuities. Frictional and tensile failure are governed by a poro-elastoplastic cohesive zone like interface law with slip-weakening friction, dilatancy, and tensile strength degradation, integrated via an elastic predictor-plastic corrector scheme. The strong nonlinear coupling between mechanical deformation and fracture permeability is handled via adaptive implicit time-stepping. Efficient block preconditioning of the coupled tangent system, leveraging hierarchical matrix representations of the boundary element operator, is essential to achieve robustness across the full range of fracture behaviors. Accuracy and convergence are demonstrated against a comprehensive suite of analytical and semi-analytical solutions of increasing complexity: fluid-driven frictional ruptures under constant and slip-weakening friction, dilatant ruptures with permeability changes, and penny shaped hydraulic fractures spanning the viscosity-to-toughness transition. The solver is further assessed on two multi-fracture configurations: injection into three intersecting fractures, and a height-confined hydraulic fracture intersecting a strike-slip fault. The proposed framework simultaneously captures frictional slip, dilatancy, permeability evolution, and tensile opening.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a fully-coupled 3D hydro-mechanical solver for simulating fluid-driven rupture propagation along existing fractures and faults, handling both frictional slip and tensile opening with complex interface laws and validated against analytical solutions and multi-fracture configurations.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of robust numerical tools that can simultaneously simulate coupled fluid flow, frictional slip, and tensile opening along arbitrary intersecting fractures in 3D, particularly for applications like hydraulic fracturing and induced seismicity.
AI.method: A coupled BEM-FEM approach (displacement discontinuity BEM for elasticity + Galerkin FEM for pore-fluid diffusion) with a poro-elastoplastic cohesive zone interface law, elastic predictor-plastic corrector integration, adaptive implicit time-stepping, and block preconditioning using hierarchical matrix representations.
AI.result: Demonstrated accuracy and convergence against analytical/semi-analytical solutions for fluid-driven frictional ruptures, dilatant ruptures, and penny-shaped hydraulic fractures, and tested on multi-fracture configurations including three intersecting fractures and a height-confined fracture intersecting a strike-slip fault.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully captures the full range of coupled behaviors (frictional slip, dilatancy, permeability evolution, tensile opening) and achieves robustness across diverse fracture regimes through efficient numerical preconditioning strategies.

title: Dimension Calculation for Spline Spaces over Rectilinear Partitions via Smoothing Cofactor Method
summary: This paper presents a general framework for calculating the dimension of spline spaces over arbitrary rectilinear partitions using the smoothing cofactor method. The approach extends existing dimension theory for polynomial splines over T-meshes by introducing the concept of TE-connected components, reducing the problem to the rank computation of explicitly constructible conformality matrices. Furthermore, a new class of rectilinear partitions, termed partitions with disjoint truncated l-edges, is introduced. It is proven that under specific conditions, the dimension of the corresponding spline space attains Schumaker's lower bound. This shows that the lower bound is attainable for arbitrary degree d and smoothness order mu in certain partition configurations. Numerical examples, including the Morgan-Scott and Yuan-Stillman partitions, validate the effectiveness and generality of the framework for both triangular and non-triangular rectilinear partitions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a general framework using smoothing cofactors to compute spline space dimensions over rectilinear partitions, introduces TE-connected components and partitions with disjoint truncated l-edges, and proves Schumaker's lower bound is attainable in certain configurations.
AI.motivation: To extend existing dimension theory for polynomial splines over T-meshes to arbitrary rectilinear partitions, addressing the gap in computing spline space dimensions for non-triangular configurations.
AI.method: Smoothing cofactor method with TE-connected components and conformality matrices; introduces a new class of rectilinear partitions (disjoint truncated l-edges).
AI.result: The dimension problem reduces to rank computation of conformality matrices; Schumaker's lower bound is attainable for arbitrary degree d and smoothness mu under certain partition conditions.
AI.conclusion: The framework is general and effective for both triangular and non-triangular rectilinear partitions, validated through numerical examples like Morgan-Scott and Yuan-Stillman partitions.

title: Numerical simulations of waves and turbulence in coronal loops: observables and spectra
summary: We investigate numerically the time evolution of velocity and magnetic field fluctuations in a coronal loop, focusing on the dynamics due to both phase mixing and turbulent cascade. The intensity, doppler velocity and non-thermal broadening are synthesized from numerical results in order to establish if the upcoming Multi-slit Solar Explorer (MUSE) mission could reveal the presence of those phenomena in the solar corona through its unprecedented high-resolution spectroscopic observations. The loop is represented by a cylindrical pressure-balanced magnetic structure with a transverse density and magnetic field inhomogeneity. The initial perturbation is a superposition of a torsional Alfvén wave and a transverse turbulent component with different tunable weights. In order to reconstruct plasma emission features we calculate moments of the Fe IX 171 Å spectral line. 2D maps obtained by integrating the emission along the assumed line of sight are calculated for the emission intensity $I_0$, the Doppler shift $I_1$ and the non-thermal broadening $I_2$, for several values of the model parameters. Finally, we simulate MUSE spectrograph by considering a resolution of $312$ km $\times$ $312$ km. We observe how intensity maps show the formation of longitudinal threads. The generation of small-scale fluctuations mainly takes place in the inhomogeneity region at the loop boundary, where the effects of phase mixing and non-thermal broadening are stronger. 1D power spectra of intensity and Doppler shift maps are calculated and compared with the corresponding spectra of density and line-of-sight velocity component. The agreement observed between the spectral indexes of the intensity power spectra at MUSE resolution and the one computed from the full 3D density field indicates that spectra of $I_0$ can be used to infer information on the spectrum of density inside a loop.

AI.tldr: This numerical study investigates turbulent cascade and phase mixing in coronal loops, simulating MUSE observations to show that intensity power spectra can reveal density fluctuation spectra inside loops.
AI.motivation: To establish whether the upcoming MUSE mission can detect phase mixing and turbulent cascade phenomena in the solar corona through its high-resolution spectroscopic observations.
AI.method: Numerical simulation of a coronal loop with transverse density/magnetic inhomogeneity, perturbed by torsional Alfvén waves and turbulence, synthesizing Fe IX 171 Å spectral line moments (intensity, Doppler shift, non-thermal broadening) at MUSE resolution (312 km).
AI.result: Intensity maps show longitudinal threads; small-scale fluctuations concentrate at the loop boundary with stronger phase mixing and non-thermal broadening; 1D power spectra of intensity at MUSE resolution match density spectra, enabling inference of density fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: MUSE observations of intensity power spectra can be used to infer the spectrum of density fluctuations inside coronal loops.

title: Some lifting and approximation properties for maps in $W^{1,2}(\mathbb{B}^3;\mathbb{S}^2)$
summary: Smooth maps $u\colon\mathbb B^3\to\mathbb S^2$ can be lifted to $\hat u\colon\mathbb B^3\to\mathbb S^3$ using the Hopf fibration $h\colon \mathbb S^3\to\mathbb S^2$ via the factorization $u=h\circ\hat u$. In this note we characterize the $W^{1,2}$-maps which have this lifting property in terms of exactness of the pullback form $u^*ω_{\mathbb S^2}$, and deduce a smooth approximation property preserving the constraint $u^*ω_{\mathbb S^2}=dη$.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes when $W^{1,2}$-maps from $\mathbb B^3$ to $\mathbb S^2$ can be lifted through the Hopf fibration to $\mathbb S^3$, linking the lifting property to exactness of the pullback area form $u^*\omega_{\mathbb S^2}$, and proves a smooth approximation result preserving the constraint $u^*\omega_{\mathbb S^2}=d\eta$.
AI.motivation: To understand which Sobolev maps admit a lift via the Hopf fibration, which is a topological and analytical question relevant to problems in harmonic maps, liquid crystals, and gauge theory.
AI.method: The paper uses the factorization $u = h \circ \hat u$ via the Hopf fibration $h:\mathbb S^3\to\mathbb S^2$ and analyzes the pullback $2$-form $u^*\omega_{\mathbb S^2}$; exactness of this form is shown to characterize the lifting property for $W^{1,2}$-maps.
AI.result: A $W^{1,2}$-map $u:\mathbb B^3\to\mathbb S^2$ can be lifted to $\hat u:\mathbb B^3\to\mathbb S^3$ if and only if $u^*\omega_{\mathbb S^2}$ is exact. Moreover, a smooth approximation result preserving the constraint $u^*\omega_{\mathbb S^2}=d\eta$ is obtained.
AI.conclusion: The exactness of the pullback area form is the key analytic condition for the Hopf lifting in the $W^{1,2}$ setting, and smooth approximations respecting this constraint exist.

title: High-Pressure Crystal Structure Database
summary: High-pressure research is a productive route to new structures and emergent properties. However, crucial high-pressure structural information remains highly fragmented across individual publications and heterogeneous computational repositories. This fragmentation creates a major bottleneck for data-driven materials design. To bridge this gap, we introduce the High-Pressure Crystal Structure Database (HPCSD), a traceable, pressure-resolved repository that integrates experimental and theoretical high-pressure structures. HPCSD is constructed from two complementary data streams: elemental high-pressure phases and a searchable configuration space of stable and metastable phases generated via CALYPSO crystal structure prediction. To ensure rigorous comparability, all retained structures underwent re-optimization under a unified density functional theory (DFT) framework , with continuous enthalpy curves systematically generated specifically for the elemental phases across their stability fields. The initial release encompasses 77,346 consistently evaluated structural entries spanning 89 elements. An analysis reveals that pressure-induced polymorphism is ubiquitous and exhibits pronounced family-dependent trends. Structural diversity is strongly influenced by an element's electronic adaptability , with the greatest structural complexity emerging at intermediate rather than highest pressures. By providing standardized, reusable, and rigorously evaluated high-pressure structural data, HPCSD establishes a robust infrastructure to accelerate experimental phase identification, facilitate cross-study thermodynamic comparisons, and support the development of machine-learning interatomic potentials and generative models for high-pressure systems.

AI.tldr: The High-Pressure Crystal Structure Database (HPCSD) integrates 77,346 experimentally and computationally derived high-pressure crystal structures across 89 elements, re-optimized under a unified DFT framework, to address data fragmentation and enable data-driven materials discovery.
AI.motivation: High-pressure structural information is scattered across individual publications and heterogeneous repositories, creating a major bottleneck for data-driven materials design and cross-study comparisons.
AI.method: HPCSD combines two data streams: (1) known elemental high-pressure phases, and (2) stable/metastable phases from CALYPSO crystal structure prediction. All structures were re-optimized under a unified DFT framework with continuous enthalpy curves generated for elemental phases.
AI.result: The database contains 77,346 consistently evaluated entries across 89 elements. Analysis shows pressure-induced polymorphism is ubiquitous with family-dependent trends, where structural complexity peaks at intermediate rather than highest pressures, influenced by electronic adaptability.
AI.conclusion: HPCSD provides standardized, reusable high-pressure structural data that accelerates phase identification, enables thermodynamic comparisons, and supports development of machine-learning potentials and generative models for high-pressure systems.

title: Iterative Methods for Computing the T-Square Root of Third-Order Tensors
summary: We develop and analyze iterative methods for computing the principal square root of third-order tensors under the T-product framework. Tensor extensions of the Newton iteration (quadratic convergence) and the Denman--Beavers iteration (geometric convergence with simultaneous computation of the inverse square root) are proposed, with rigorous convergence guarantees established via the Fourier-domain block-diagonalization of the T-product. We apply these methods to image processing, introducing Tensor Decorrelated Grayscale conversion, T-Whitening, and optimal color transfer under the T-product geometry. We also formulate the Tensor Bures--Wasserstein distance and prove it defines a valid metric on the space of T-positive definite tensors. Numerical experiments confirm rapid convergence and demonstrate that the proposed tensor-based techniques offer improved structural preservation and cross-channel decorrelation compared to classical methods.

AI.tldr: This paper develops iterative methods (Newton and Denman–Beavers) for computing the principal square root of third-order tensors under the T-product framework, with applications to image processing (decorrelated grayscale conversion, whitening, color transfer) and the introduction of a Tensor Bures–Wasserstein distance metric.
AI.motivation: To extend classical matrix square root iterations to third-order tensors under the T-product framework, enabling tensor-level operations that preserve cross-channel structure in multi-dimensional data (e.g., color images).
AI.method: Proposes Newton iteration (quadratic convergence) and Denman–Beavers iteration (geometric convergence with inverse square root) for tensors, leveraging Fourier-domain block-diagonalization of the T-product for convergence guarantees. Applies these to image tasks: Tensor Decorrelated Grayscale conversion, T-Whitening, optimal color transfer, and defines the Tensor Bures–Wasserstein distance.
AI.result: Numerical experiments confirm rapid convergence. Tensor-based techniques demonstrate improved structural preservation and cross-channel decorrelation compared to classical methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tensor square root iterations are effective and provide a principled geometric framework for tensor-valued data processing, with the Tensor Bures–Wasserstein distance forming a valid metric on T-positive definite tensors.

title: Axion magnetohydrodynamics and reconnection-driven axion bursts
comment: Axion magnetohydrodynamics, magnetars, axion bursts, sensitivity projection
summary: We formulate axion magnetohydrodynamics beyond the ideal limit, retaining axion inertia and the essential physics of non-ideal plasmas from first principles. In this framework, regions where magnetic flux freezing breaks down acquire a new physical role: whenever $\mathbf{E} \cdot\ \mathbf{B} \neq 0$, magnetic dissipation acts as a localized source of axion radiation. We show that magnetic reconnection naturally excites mixed Alfvén-axion modes, enabling coherent energy exchange between magnetic fields and axions in magnetically dominated environments. In neutron stars and magnetars, this mechanism leads generically to transient axion bursts powered by reconnection--driven Alfvénic dissipation. We connect this production process to observational prospects and derive a characteristic sensitivity to the axion--photon coupling, complementary to searches based on static magnetic fields.

AI.tldr: This paper extends axion magnetohydrodynamics beyond the ideal limit, showing that magnetic reconnection in non-ideal plasmas generates axion radiation, particularly through mixed Alfvén-axion modes in neutron stars and magnetars, producing transient axion bursts that offer new observational signatures.
AI.motivation: To understand axion production in astrophysical plasmas beyond static field approximations, especially in dynamic, non-ideal environments where magnetic reconnection occurs.
AI.method: Formulates axion magnetohydrodynamics from first principles including non-ideal plasma effects and axion inertia, analyzing how magnetic dissipation sources axion radiation.
AI.result: Shows that magnetic reconnection excites mixed Alfvén-axion modes, leading to transient axion bursts in neutron stars and magnetars via reconnection-driven Alfvénic dissipation.
AI.conclusion: This mechanism provides a complementary observational signature for axion-photon coupling, distinct from searches using static magnetic fields.

title: Runaway avalanches in plasmas with external electric fields: spatially inhomogeneous case in a perturbation framework
summary:
We consider the Landau-Coulomb equation for a (hydrogen) plasma heated by an external electric field. In this setting, theoretical and experimental results in plasma physics show the emergence of so-called \emph{runaway electrons} which are linearly accelerating but only lead to a minimal increase of the plasma temperature. Runaway electrons are a major obstacle in nuclear fusion since they can overcome the confinement and damage the structure of the reactor.
  We rigorously prove the well-posedness of the underlying nonlinear \emph{open} Landau-Coulomb system in a perturbative setting and the conjectured growth bounds for the mean velocity and plasma temperature. We show that the mean velocity is linearly increasing in time, and capture the sharp logarithmic growth of the temperature. Furthermore, we prove that the electron distribution can be asymptotically described by a scattering-type Maxwellian. Due to the different nature of the electron-electron and electron-ion interactions, we recast the equation as a novel coupled system that allows us to isolate the dissipation structures of the two operators. For the coupled system, we perform a micro-macro decomposition to show convergence to the scattering-type Maxwellian.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously proves well-posedness of the Landau-Coulomb equation for a plasma with runaway electrons, showing linear velocity growth and sharp logarithmic temperature increase, with asymptotic convergence to a scattering-type Maxwellian.
AI.motivation: To rigorously understand runaway electrons in nuclear fusion plasmas, which can damage reactor structures, by proving theoretically observed growth bounds on mean velocity and temperature.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the system to isolate electron-electron and electron-ion dissipation structures, then apply micro-macro decomposition to prove convergence to scattering-type Maxwellian.
AI.result: Proven well-posedness of the nonlinear open Landau-Coulomb system, linear increase of mean velocity over time, sharp logarithmic temperature growth, and asymptotic scattering-type Maxwellian behavior.
AI.conclusion: The analysis validates the runaway electron phenomenon mathematically and provides rigorous bounds confirming plasma physics predictions.

title: Lang2MLIP: End-to-End Language-to-Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Development with Autonomous Agentic Workflows
summary: Developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for complex materials systems remains challenging because it requires expertise in atomistic simulations, machine learning, and workflow design, as well as iterative active learning procedures. Existing automated pipelines typically assume a fixed sequence of stages or depend on domain experts, which limits their adaptability to heterogeneous materials systems where the optimal curriculum is not known in advance. To lower the barrier to developing MLIPs for non-experts, we propose Lang2MLIP, a multi-agent framework that takes natural-language input and formulates end-to-end MLIP development as a sequential decision-making problem solved by large language models (LLMs). At each step, a decision-making agent observes the current dataset, model, evaluation results, and execution log, and then automatically selects an appropriate action to improve the model. This removes the need for a predefined pipeline and enables the agent to self-correct by revisiting earlier subsystems when new failures arise. We evaluate this approach on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces. These results suggest that LLM-based multi-agent systems are a promising direction for automating MLIP development and making it more accessible to non-experts.

AI.tldr: Lang2MLIP is a multi-agent LLM framework that automates MLIP development from natural language input, treating it as a sequential decision-making problem without requiring predefined pipelines or expert intervention.
AI.motivation: Developing MLIPs for complex materials is challenging and requires expertise across multiple domains; existing automated pipelines are rigid and assume fixed stages, making them poorly adaptable to heterogeneous systems where optimal curricula are unknown.
AI.method: A multi-agent LLM framework that takes natural language input, frames MLIP development as sequential decision-making, and uses a decision-making agent to observe current state (dataset, model, evaluation, logs) and select actions to iteratively improve the model without a predefined pipeline.
AI.result: Evaluated on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces, demonstrating the framework's effectiveness on complex heterogeneous materials.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based multi-agent systems are a promising direction for automating MLIP development and making it more accessible to non-experts.

title: Eigenbounds of symmetric positive definite tensors
summary: This article introduces an algebraic framework for establishing eigenvalue bounds for symmetric positive definite tensors by leveraging intrinsic invariants, specifically the trace and determinant (resultant). We derive a hierarchy of inequalities via the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean (AM-GM) inequality that yields progressively tighter upper and lower bounds for the tensor spectral radius and smallest eigenvalue. A comprehensive comparative analysis demonstrates that our invariant-based approach significantly outperforms classical coordinate-dependent methods such as the Gershgorin circle theorem. We explicitly show that our bounds remain robust and informative in scenarios where Gershgorin bounds fail, particularly for tensors with negative off-diagonal entries, where algebraic cancellations occur, and higher-order tensors, where combinatorial growth leads to loose estimates. Furthermore, we validate the practical utility of these bounds by applying them to certify the positive definiteness of Lyapunov functions in the stability analysis of nonlinear autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an algebraic framework using trace and determinant invariants with AM-GM inequalities to derive tight eigenvalue bounds for symmetric positive definite tensors, outperforming classical Gershgorin methods, especially for tensors with negative off-diagonals and higher-order cases, with applications to Lyapunov stability analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing coordinate-dependent methods like Gershgorin's circle theorem fail to provide reliable eigenvalue bounds for tensors with negative off-diagonal entries or high-order tensors due to combinatorial growth and algebraic cancellations. An invariant-based approach is needed.
AI.method: The authors introduce an algebraic framework leveraging intrinsic invariants (trace and determinant/resultant) of symmetric positive definite tensors, then apply the AM-GM inequality to derive a hierarchy of progressively tighter upper and lower bounds for the spectral radius and smallest eigenvalue.
AI.result: The invariant-based bounds significantly outperform classical Gershgorin bounds, remaining robust and informative in scenarios where Gershgorin fails. The method certifies positive definiteness of Lyapunov functions in nonlinear autonomous system stability analysis.
AI.conclusion: Invariant-based eigenvalue bounds provide a powerful, coordinate-free alternative to classical methods, with demonstrated practical utility in stability certification for nonlinear dynamical systems.

title: Optimal Weighted Smoothing and Asymptotics of Ancient Solutions for Fast Diffusion Equations
summary:
We establish sharp weighted smoothing estimates for limit solutions to the Cauchy-Dirichlet problem for the fast diffusion equation on smooth bounded domains.
  We demonstrate that the critical exponent governing these estimates coincides with the classical Brezis--Turner exponent known in the theory of semilinear elliptic equations.
  As a primary application, we derive improved global Harnack inequalities and describe asymptotic behavior of positive ancient solutions.

AI.tldr: Sharp weighted smoothing estimates are established for limit solutions of the fast diffusion equation on bounded domains, revealing a critical exponent matching the Brezis–Turner exponent from semilinear elliptic theory, with applications to improved Harnack inequalities and the asymptotic behavior of ancient solutions.
AI.motivation: To understand the regularity and asymptotic behavior of solutions to the fast diffusion equation, particularly establishing sharp smoothing estimates and their connection to known elliptic critical exponents.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the Cauchy-Dirichlet problem for the fast diffusion equation on smooth bounded domains, deriving weighted smoothing estimates and identifying the critical exponent that corresponds to the Brezis–Turner exponent from semilinear elliptic equations.
AI.result: Sharp weighted smoothing estimates are obtained, with the critical exponent shown to match the classical Brezis–Turner exponent. These lead to improved global Harnack inequalities and a characterization of the asymptotic behavior of positive ancient solutions.
AI.conclusion: The results bridge parabolic (fast diffusion) and elliptic (Brezis–Turner) critical exponents, yielding sharper Harnack inequalities and a complete description of ancient solution asymptotics on bounded domains.

title: N-Graphdiyne as a Tunable Platform for Stabilizing Light Metals toward High-Capacity Reversible Hydrogen Storage
summary: Hydrogen (H2) is a promising carbon-neutral energy carrier. However, its deployment is limited by the lack of lightweight, reversible storage media that operate under practical conditions. Here, we establish nitrogen-doped graphdiyne (N-GDY) as a programmable two-dimensional platform for stabilizing dispersed light-metal dopants and enabling high-capacity physisorption of molecular H2. The computational package involves density functional theory (DFT) combined with ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) and Langmuir-based statistical thermodynamic modeling. The results revealed that N-sites of N-GDY bind up to five Li, Na, K, and Ca atoms per primitive cell with binding energies of -2.27, -1.57, -1.80, and -2.13 eV, respectively, exceeding their respective bulk cohesive energies. AIMD simulations at 400 K further confirm the structural robustness of the decorated frameworks and the absence of metal aggregation. The polarised metal centres activate reversible H2 adsorption through electrostatic and dispersion interactions, with average adsorption energies falling within the optimal window (-0.15 to -0.35 eV per H2). Sequential adsorption analysis reveals uptake of up to 25 H2 molecules per primitive cell, achieving intrinsic gravimetric capacities of 13.08, 10.82, 9.23, and 9.15 wt% for Li-, Na-, K-, and Ca-functionalized systems, respectively. Thermodynamic analysis indicates favorable adsorption-desorption behavior under near-ambient conditions, with Li- and Ca-functionalized systems exceeding the 6.5 wt% U.S. Department of Energy's ultimate system-level target when considering intrinsic material capacity. These results identify N-GDY as a chemically tunable scaffold for dispersing lightweight metals and provide a mechanistic design strategy for achieving high-capacity, reversible hydrogen storage in porous two-dimensional materials.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates nitrogen-doped graphdiyne (N-GDY) as a programmable 2D platform for dispersing light-metal dopants (Li, Na, K, Ca) that enables high-capacity reversible hydrogen storage, achieving up to 13.08 wt% gravimetric capacity via combined DFT, AIMD, and thermodynamic modeling.
AI.motivation: Hydrogen storage lacks lightweight, reversible media that work under practical conditions; this work aims to design a material platform overcoming metal aggregation and low capacity issues.
AI.method: DFT, ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD), and Langmuir-based statistical thermodynamic modeling to study N-GDY decorated with Li, Na, K, and Ca atoms.
AI.result: N-sites bind up to 5 metal atoms per cell with binding energies exceeding bulk cohesive energies; up to 25 H2 molecules per cell adsorbed; intrinsic capacities of 13.08, 10.82, 9.23, and 9.15 wt% for Li, Na, K, Ca systems; Li and Ca exceed DOE's 6.5 wt% ultimate target.
AI.conclusion: N-GDY is a chemically tunable scaffold for dispersing lightweight metals, providing a mechanistic design strategy for high-capacity, reversible hydrogen storage in porous 2D materials.

title: A Least-Squares Weak Galerkin Finite Element Scheme for Cauchy Problems in Convection--Diffusion
summary: We introduce and rigorously analyze a least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) finite element method for the severely ill-posed Cauchy problem of convection--diffusion equations. The proposed framework utilizes weak derivatives defined on a class of discontinuous weak functions, enabling the natural treatment of complex boundary conditions and internal interfaces. A key advantage of the least-squares formulation is that it transforms the underlying non-self-adjoint operator into a discrete linear system that is inherently symmetric and positive definite (SPD). We demonstrate the geometric flexibility of the method on arbitrary polygonal and polyhedral partitions. Furthermore, we establish the uniqueness of the numerical solution and derive optimal-order error estimates in a carefully defined discrete energy norm. Extensive numerical tests are presented to confirm the theoretical convergence rates and highlight the algorithm's robustness and efficiency compared to standard Galerkin approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) finite element method for solving severely ill-posed Cauchy problems of convection-diffusion equations, featuring symmetric positive definite linear systems, geometric flexibility on arbitrary polygonal/polyhedral partitions, and proven optimal-order error estimates validated by numerical tests.
AI.motivation: To address the severely ill-posed Cauchy problem for convection-diffusion equations, which standard Galerkin methods struggle with due to the non-self-adjoint nature of the operator and complex boundary conditions.
AI.method: A least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) finite element method using weak derivatives on discontinuous weak functions, transforming the non-self-adjoint operator into a symmetric positive definite discrete linear system.
AI.result: The method yields an SPD system, demonstrates geometric flexibility on arbitrary polygonal/polyhedral meshes, establishes uniqueness and optimal-order error estimates in a discrete energy norm, and numerical tests confirm convergence rates and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The LS-WG method is a robust, efficient, and theoretically sound approach for ill-posed Cauchy problems of convection-diffusion equations, outperforming standard Galerkin methods.

title: Bilinear embedding for divergence-form operators with first-order terms and negative potentials
summary:
This article establishes a bilinear embedding for second-order divergence-form operators with complex coefficients, characterized by the simultaneous presence of first-order terms and negative potentials. This work provides a further development of the theory initiated by Carbonaro and Dragičević for the homogeneous case, and recently extended by the second author to cases where first-order terms or negative potentials were treated in isolation.
  We work in the general setting of arbitrary open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ under Dirichlet, Neumann, or mixed boundary conditions. Our main contribution is the introduction of a unified notion of generalized $p$-ellipticity that extends all its predecessors and serves as the natural condition for the bilinear inequality. Methodologically, we overcome the rigidity of the Bellman-heat method on arbitrary open subsets by introducing a novel sequence-based approach that unifies and simplifies the previous techniques.
  As fundamental applications, we prove the boundedness of the $H^\infty$-calculus on $L^p$ and establish $L^p$-maximal regularity. Moreover, we show that this generalized $p$-ellipticity provides a sufficient condition for the $L^p$-contractivity and $L^p$-analyticity of the generated semigroup.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a bilinear embedding for second-order divergence-form operators with complex coefficients, first-order terms, and negative potentials on arbitrary open subsets of ℝ^d. It introduces generalized p-ellipticity as a unified condition, uses a novel sequence-based approach to overcome limitations of prior methods, and proves H^∞-calculus boundedness, L^p-maximal regularity, and L^p-contractivity/analyticity of the semigroup.
AI.motivation: To extend the theory of bilinear embeddings for divergence-form operators from the homogeneous case (Carbonaro & Dragičević) to operators simultaneously involving first-order terms and negative potentials, and to unify previously isolated treatments under a generalized notion of p-ellipticity.
AI.method: Introduces a sequence-based approach that overcomes the rigidity of the Bellman-heat method on arbitrary open subsets, enabling a unified and simplified treatment of Dirichlet, Neumann, or mixed boundary conditions.
AI.result: Establishes the bilinear inequality under generalized p-ellipticity, proves boundedness of H^∞-calculus on L^p, L^p-maximal regularity, and shows that generalized p-ellipticity is sufficient for L^p-contractivity and L^p-analyticity of the semigroup.
AI.conclusion: The unified notion of generalized p-ellipticity serves as a natural condition for the bilinear embedding, enabling broad functional analytic results for complex second-order operators with perturbations.

title: Functional and Density-Driven Errors in Density Functional Theory: Quantum Monte Carlo Benchmarks for Solids
