summary: We introduce a systematic analysis of density functional approximation errors in solids by separating functional-driven from density-driven contributions using quantum Monte Carlo densities of silicon, sodium chloride, and copper as reference. Typically, functional errors dominate, but we identify important exceptions where density-driven errors exceed functional errors by factors of 2-3, notably for SOGGA11 and τ-HCTH in the semiconductor and the insulator. Material dependence is striking: 63% of functionals show error cancellation in silicon versus 18% in copper, and only five functionals surpass LDA accuracy for metallic copper even with exact densities. For silicon and sodium chloride, GILL or BECKE exchange combined with PBE, PW91, or P86 correlation achieves near-exact xc energies on QMC densities, while copper requires specialized functionals like PBEsol or PBELYP. High-quality densities consistently reduce density-driven errors across all systems. Historical analysis reveals that 1990s GGA functionals outperform many modern meta-GGAs, contradicting expectations of systematic improvement along Jacob's ladder. These results provide practical guidance for functional selection and highlight implications for machine learning potential development, where material-dependent error cancellation may compromise transferability.

AI.tldr: This paper separates functional-driven from density-driven errors in DFT for solids using QMC reference densities, finding density-driven errors dominate for some functionals, strong material dependence in error cancellation, and that 1990s GGAs often outperform modern meta-GGAs.
AI.motivation: To systematically identify when density-driven errors (from inaccurate densities) versus functional-driven errors (from the exchange-correlation functional itself) dominate in solid-state DFT calculations, and to understand material-specific performance.
AI.method: Use quantum Monte Carlo densities as reference for silicon, NaCl, and copper; separate density-driven from functional-driven errors; evaluate 63 functionals across these solids.
AI.result: Density-driven errors exceed functional errors by 2-3× for SOGGA11 and τ-HCTH in Si/NaCl; 63% of functionals show error cancellation in Si vs 18% in Cu; only 5 functionals beat LDA for Cu even with exact densities; 1990s GGAs outperform many modern meta-GGAs.
AI.conclusion: High-quality densities reduce errors; material-dependent error cancellation challenges transferability in ML potentials; functional choice should consider both density and functional accuracy.

title: Nyström Approximation on Manifolds
summary: Computations on a manifold often involve constructing an operator on the tangent space and computing its inverse, which can be time-consuming in many applications. In order to reduce the computational costs and preserve the benign properties of tangent operators, we develop the Riemannian Nyström approximation on manifolds, a low-rank approximation of tangent operators through subspace projections onto the tangent space. The developed approximation is intrinsically constructed and inherits desirable properties from the classical Nyström approximation, e.g., positive semidefiniteness and approximation errors. Instead of the Gaussian sketching, we introduce the Haar--Grassmann sketching condition with a coordinate-free representation, which remains compatible under isometric vector transport across tangent spaces. Moreover, we propose a randomized Newton-type method for optimization on manifolds in which the linear system is constructed via the Riemannian Nyström approximation. Numerical experiments on the SPD and Grassmann manifolds, together with principal geodesic analysis on real data, illustrate that the proposed approximation reduces the computational cost of operators while maintaining comparable accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a Riemannian Nyström approximation for low-rank approximation of tangent operators on manifolds, introducing a Haar-Grassmann sketching condition and applying it to a randomized Newton-type optimization method, achieving computational savings with maintained accuracy.
AI.motivation: Computing and inverting tangent operators on manifolds is computationally expensive. The authors aim to reduce these costs while preserving the benign properties of tangent operators.
AI.method: 1) Riemannian Nyström approximation via subspace projections onto the tangent space. 2) Haar-Grassmann sketching with coordinate-free representation compatible under isometric vector transport. 3) Randomized Newton-type method for optimization on manifolds using the proposed approximation.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on SPD and Grassmann manifolds, plus principal geodesic analysis on real data, show the approximation reduces computational cost of operators while maintaining comparable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The Riemannian Nyström approximation successfully provides a low-rank, computationally efficient surrogate for tangent operators on manifolds, preserving positive semidefiniteness and error bounds, and enables faster Riemannian optimization.

title: Weak Solutions and Inertial Limits for Quasi-static Filtrations
summary: A quasi-static filtration system, comprising a poroelastic solid coupled to an incompressible free-flow, is considered in 3D. Across a flat 2D interface, the Beavers-Joseph-Saffman coupling conditions are taken. The system constitutes a doubly elliptic-parabolic coupling and can be seen as a degenerate case of the inertial Biot-Stokes dynamics of recent interest. These dynamics cannot be easily recovered through a vanishing inertia limit, however, utilizing a viscoelastic regularization of the inertial Biot system allows us to construct weak solutions in the inertial limit; subsequently, we pass to the limit in the regularization parameter to obtain quasi-static weak solutions. This addresses an open singular/degenerate limiting problem in filtrations, and allows for subsequent analysis of uniqueness and regularity. This also provides a foundation for the incorporation of physically-motivated nonlinear poroelastic effects.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs weak solutions for a 3D quasi-static poroelastic-free-flow system with Beavers-Joseph-Saffman coupling, overcoming singular/degenerate limits via viscoelastic regularization of inertial Biot-Stokes dynamics.
AI.motivation: To address an open singular/degenerate limiting problem in filtration systems where quasi-static Biot-Stokes dynamics cannot be easily recovered through a vanishing inertia limit.
AI.method: Using a viscoelastic regularization of the inertial Biot system to construct weak solutions in the inertial limit, then passing to the limit in the regularization parameter to obtain quasi-static weak solutions.
AI.result: Successfully constructs quasi-static weak solutions for the doubly elliptic-parabolic coupled system, addressing the open singular/degenerate limiting problem.
AI.conclusion: This work enables subsequent analysis of uniqueness and regularity, and provides a foundation for incorporating physically-motivated nonlinear poroelastic effects.

title: Lévy-like flights and fractal geometry of finite point sets
summary: We study Lévy-like and truncated Lévy-like flights with step probability distribution of the form $r^{-1+ν}$ for negative, positive, and zero $ν$, focusing on the appearance of fractal geometry characteristics in the generated point sets. Forming ensembles of such point sets with fixed multiplicity, we develop simulation techniques leading to the desired value of correlation dimension in a vast continuous interval of scales. In particular, we demonstrate the possibility to produce ensembles of data sets with a low number of points with the needed properties. Furthermore, we show that the positive $ν$ distributions, apart from a region near the upper scale limit, show fractal behaviour that extends to infinitesimally low scales. As an example, we apply our findings to producing simulations relevant to the search for critical fluctuations, related to QCD critical endpoint, in heavy-ion collision experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Lévy-like and truncated Lévy-like flights with step distributions r^{-1+ν} for various ν, develops simulation techniques to achieve desired correlation dimensions across scales, and applies findings to simulations for QCD critical endpoint searches in heavy-ion collisions.
AI.motivation: To understand fractal geometry characteristics in point sets generated by Lévy-like flights, and to enable simulations relevant to critical fluctuation searches in heavy-ion collision experiments, particularly related to the QCD critical endpoint.
AI.method: The authors study step probability distributions of the form r^{-1+ν} for negative, positive, and zero ν, develop simulation techniques to achieve desired correlation dimensions across a continuous interval of scales, and form ensembles of point sets with fixed multiplicity.
AI.result: The method can produce ensembles of data sets with low numbers of points having needed properties. Positive ν distributions show fractal behavior extending to infinitesimally low scales, except near the upper scale limit.
AI.conclusion: The developed simulation techniques enable producing point sets with controlled fractal properties, applicable to simulating critical fluctuations for QCD critical endpoint searches in heavy-ion collision experiments.

title: Adaptive homotopy continuation for robust dispersion curve computation in viscoelastic waveguides: guaranteed branch identity continuity
summary: This paper presents the first systematic application of a material homotopy continuation framework for efficient, automated computation of dispersion curves in viscoelastic waveguides of arbitrary cross-section. A material homotopy continuously maps the original lossy problem to an auxiliary lossless one via an attenuation parameter s in [0,1], addressing the core challenges of the non-Hermitian eigenvalue problem. Grounded in analytic perturbation theory, the method guarantees branch identity continuity--a one-to-one correspondence between solutions at s=0 and s=1--provided the real-parameter path does not cross any exceptional points. Under a Type I exceptional point topology, physical mode labels established at the elastic stage remain valid at the viscoelastic stage without post-processing, yielding the characteristic real-part veering with imaginary-part crossing. The decoupling strategy performs reliable mode tracking in the Hermitian regime via adaptive wavenumber refinement, then propagates a sparse set of key solutions to the target viscoelastic state through predictor-corrector homotopy continuation. Numerical examples across symmetric and unsymmetric laminates validate the framework's robustness and efficiency, with the majority of cases verified at a loss factor of approximately 0.003 and a single symmetric laminate providing additional support at 0.02. For a challenging unsymmetric laminate at a loss factor of 0.05, the method still produces numerically accurate solutions; two complementary diagnostic signatures--an extremely sharp imaginary-part crossing and a discernible discrepancy between spectral group velocity and energy flux velocity--warn of potential label mismatch and guide further analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a material homotopy continuation framework for computing dispersion curves in viscoelastic waveguides, using an attenuation parameter to map lossy problems to lossless ones while guaranteeing mode label continuity via analytic perturbation theory.
AI.motivation: To address the core challenges of non-Hermitian eigenvalue problems in viscoelastic waveguides, specifically enabling efficient, automated computation of dispersion curves without losing track of mode identities.
AI.method: A material homotopy continuously maps the lossy problem to a lossless one via an attenuation parameter s∈[0,1]. It uses Type I exceptional point topology to ensure physical mode labels from the elastic stage remain valid at the viscoelastic stage. The decoupling strategy performs mode tracking in the Hermitian regime via adaptive wavenumber refinement, then propagates solutions to the target viscoelastic state through predictor-corrector homotopy continuation.
AI.result: Validated across symmetric and unsymmetric laminates at loss factors of ~0.003, 0.02, and 0.05. The method produces numerically accurate solutions even for challenging unsymmetric laminates, with diagnostic signatures (sharp imaginary-part crossing, spectral vs. energy flux velocity discrepancy) warning of potential label mismatch.
AI.conclusion: The material homotopy continuation framework offers robust and efficient dispersion curve computation for viscoelastic waveguides with guaranteed branch identity continuity, and provides diagnostic tools for detecting label mismatches in challenging cases.

title: On the Necessity of Logarithmic Estimates for Hypoellipticity
summary: This paper is focused on necessary conditions for hypoellipticity of an operator $L$ of the form $L=L_1(x)+g(x)L_2(y)$, where the operator $L_1$ is either elliptic or parabolic, $L_2$ is degenerately elliptic and $g(x)$ may itself vanish adding further degeneracy. First, we establish a logarithmic criterion: if the operator $L$ above is hypoelliptic and $L_1$ has a family of spectral solutions we define in the paper, then the remaining part $L_2$ must gain a power of a logarithm of a derivative. Such a property can be thought of as a restriction on degeneracy of the operator $L_2$. We then use this criterion to examine degenerate elliptic and parabolic operators closing gaps between sufficiency and necessity that have been open since 1980s in three and higher dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes necessary conditions for hypoellipticity of operators combining elliptic/parabolic and degenerate elliptic components, introducing a logarithmic criterion that relates to how many powers of a logarithm of a derivative the degenerate part must gain, and applies it to resolve open problems from the 1980s in dimensions three and higher.
AI.motivation: To establish necessary conditions for hypoellipticity of a class of operators L = L₁(x) + g(x)L₂(y), and to close longstanding gaps between sufficiency and necessity conditions for degenerate elliptic and parabolic operators that have remained open since the 1980s in three and higher dimensions.
AI.method: A logarithmic criterion is derived: if the operator L is hypoelliptic and L₁ has a family of spectral solutions, then L₂ must gain a power of a logarithm of a derivative, which acts as a restriction on the degeneracy of L₂.
AI.result: A necessary logarithmic criterion for hypoellipticity is established, and it is used to close gaps between sufficiency and necessity conditions for degenerate elliptic and parabolic operators in dimensions three and higher—problems that had been unresolved since the 1980s.
AI.conclusion: The logarithmic criterion provides a refined necessary condition for hypoellipticity, enabling the resolution of long-standing open problems in higher dimensions concerning degenerate elliptic and parabolic operators.

title: A Monte Carlo positronium decay source model with multiple annihilation channels in GATE
summary:
Positronium-based imaging requires realistic modelling of positronium (Ps) decay in matter. We introduce a modular Ps decay model implemented in GATE 9.4 and GATE 10, enabling the definition of an arbitrary number of decay channels characterised by lifetime, branching fraction, annihilation multiplicity (2g/3g), and optional prompt photon emission. The model is validated through analytical and numerical benchmarks, including lifetime distributions, branching fraction consistency, photon kinematics, and prompt photon emission. Its practical applicability is demonstrated using simulations of mixed annihilation scenarios and the NEMA IEC phantom with a large field-of-view PET system. The proposed model accurately reproduces input lifetime distributions as weighted sums of exponential components and correctly samples decay channel fractions. Simulated two- and three-photon annihilation kinematics are consistent with theoretical expectations. Complex mixtures of decay channels, including varying 3g-to-2g ratios and multi-component ortho-positronium lifetimes, are correctly modelled, with observable signatures reflected in both temporal and energy distributions. Phantom simulations demonstrate the capability to generate realistic positronium-sensitive datasets.
  This work provides the first general-purpose, multi-channel positronium decay model integrated into GATE, enabling realistic simulations of positronium behaviour in complex media. The model supports the development and optimisation of positronium-based imaging techniques, including PLI and multi-photon PET, and applies to medical imaging, industrial tomography, and fundamental physics studies. Its public availability and compatibility with standard GATE workflows make it a valuable tool for the broader research community.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a modular, multi-channel positronium (Ps) decay model implemented in GATE 9.4 and GATE 10, enabling realistic simulations of Ps decay with customizable decay channels, lifetimes, branching fractions, and photon multiplicities. The model is validated analytically and through phantom simulations, supporting positronium-based imaging techniques like PLI and multi-photon PET.
AI.motivation: Positronium-based imaging requires realistic modeling of positronium decay in matter, but no general-purpose, multi-channel Ps decay model was previously integrated into GATE for simulating complex decay scenarios.
AI.method: A modular Ps decay model was developed in GATE 9.4 and GATE 10, supporting arbitrary decay channels with configurable lifetime, branching fraction, annihilation multiplicity (2g/3g), and optional prompt photon emission. Validation was performed via analytical benchmarks, numerical tests, and phantom simulations using the NEMA IEC phantom with a large field-of-view PET system.
AI.result: The model accurately reproduces input lifetime distributions, correctly samples decay channel fractions, and yields simulated two- and three-photon annihilation kinematics consistent with theoretical expectations. Complex mixtures of decay channels are correctly modeled with observable signatures in temporal and energy distributions.
AI.conclusion: This is the first general-purpose, multi-channel Ps decay model integrated into GATE, enabling realistic simulations for positronium-based imaging (PLI, multi-photon PET), industrial tomography, and fundamental physics studies. The model is publicly available and compatible with standard GATE workflows.

title: Scalable Mechanistic Neural Networks for Differential Equations and Machine Learning
comment: Published as a conference paper at the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2025): https://openreview.net/forum?id=Oazgf8A24z
summary: We propose Scalable Mechanistic Neural Network (S-MNN), an enhanced neural network framework designed for scientific machine learning applications involving long temporal sequences. By reformulating the original Mechanistic Neural Network (MNN) (Pervez et al., 2024), we reduce the computational time and space complexities from cubic and quadratic with respect to the sequence length, respectively, to linear. This significant improvement enables efficient modeling of long-term dynamics without sacrificing accuracy or interpretability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that S-MNN matches the original MNN in precision while substantially reducing computational resources. Consequently, S-MNN can drop-in replace the original MNN in applications, providing a practical and efficient tool for integrating mechanistic bottlenecks into neural network models of complex dynamical systems. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/ScalableMNN.

AI.tldr: S-MNN improves the Mechanistic Neural Network by reducing time/space complexity from cubic/quadratic to linear in sequence length, enabling efficient long-sequence scientific ML while maintaining accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: The original MNN had cubic time and quadratic space complexity with respect to sequence length, making it impractical for long temporal sequences in scientific machine learning.
AI.method: Reformulates the original MNN architecture to achieve linear time and space complexity while preserving mechanistic bottlenecks and interpretability.
AI.result: S-MNN matches the original MNN in precision but requires substantially less computational resources, enabling efficient long-term dynamics modeling.
AI.conclusion: S-MNN serves as a drop-in replacement for MNN, providing a practical and efficient tool for scientific ML with complex dynamical systems.

title: An inverse problem for inhomogeneous Signorini obstacle
summary: This paper investigates the inverse problem of determining a general Signorini obstacle using boundary measurements. We demonstrate that both the shape of the obstacle and the obstacle function can be uniquely determined from solution measurements taken on an arbitrary open subset of the boundary. This result applies to both the scalar and elasticity versions of the Signorini problem.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that both the shape and function of a Signorini obstacle can be uniquely determined from boundary measurements, applicable to scalar and elasticity problems.
AI.motivation: To solve the inverse problem of determining a general Signorini obstacle (both shape and obstacle function) using only boundary measurement data.
AI.method: The authors employ boundary measurement techniques, taking solution measurements from an arbitrary open subset of the boundary to infer obstacle properties.
AI.result: Both the obstacle's shape and the obstacle function can be uniquely determined from boundary measurements on any open subset of the boundary.
AI.conclusion: The inverse Signorini obstacle problem has a unique solution for both scalar and elasticity formulations, using only partial boundary data.

title: Characterization of stability radii for robustly asymptotically stable dissipative Hamiltonian differential-algebraic systems
summary:
We study linear time-invariant dissipative Hamiltonian differential-algebraic systems.
  We characterize when the systems are robustly asymptotically stable and derive exact conditions and bounds when this property is lost under structure-preserving perturbations.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes robust asymptotic stability of linear time-invariant dissipative Hamiltonian differential-algebraic systems and derives exact conditions/bounds for stability loss under structure-preserving perturbations.
AI.motivation: To understand when dissipative Hamiltonian differential-algebraic systems remain asymptotically stable under perturbations, and to quantify when this stability property is lost.
AI.method: Linear time-invariant dissipative Hamiltonian differential-algebraic systems analysis with characterization of robust asymptotic stability, deriving exact conditions and bounds for stability loss under structure-preserving perturbations.
AI.result: Exact conditions and bounds for when robust asymptotic stability is lost under structure-preserving perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous characterization of robust stability and quantifiable measures for stability loss in this class of systems.

title: Asymptotically Self-Similar Blowup for 3D Incompressible Euler with $C^{1, 1/3-}$ Velocity II: 3D Profiles, Blowup, and Limiting behavior
summary: For any $α\in (0, 1/3)$, we construct exact $C^{1,α}$ self-similar blowup profiles for the 3D incompressible Euler equation without swirl, and build on them to prove asymptotically self-similar blowup from $C_c^α$ initial vorticity and $C^{1,α}\cap L^2$ initial velocity. Moreover, we provide a complete characterization of the limiting behavior of the $C^{1,α}$ blowup profiles and the associated $C^{1,α}$ blowup solutions as $α\to(1/3)^-$. Specifically, as $α\to(1/3)^-$, the spatial blowup rate $c_{x,α}$ diverges to $\infty$, while the $C^{1,α}$ blowup profile $Ω_{*,α}^θ$ asymptotically factorizes and converges strongly in a weighted $L^\infty$ norm to a nonzero constant multiple of $r^{1/3} \bar W_{1/3 }(z)$, where $ \bar W_{1/3}$ is a $C^\infty$ 1D blowup profile. Our construction is inspired by the Hou--Zhang blowup scenario. Using a fixed-point argument, we lift the $C^\infty$ blowup profiles for a 1D model constructed in the companion work [11] to exact 3D blowup profiles. To overcome the lack of $r$-directional decay in the approximate profile and capture the anisotropic structure, we develop a family of anisotropic weighted estimates and introduce a crucial integration-by-parts method along trajectories that exploits the equation twice. We then develop a finite codimension stability argument in a low-regularity setting to prove stability of the 3D profiles and establish asymptotically self-similar blowup. This blowup result is sharp in view of the global regularity theory for axisymmetric Euler without swirl with $C^{1,1/3+}\cap L^2$ initial velocity. To the best of our knowledge, our results provide the first example in which a singularity from a 1D nonlocal fluid model is lifted to construct blowup for incompressible fluid equations in $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$.

AI.tldr: For α∈(0,1/3), the authors construct C^{1,α} self-similar blowup profiles for the 3D incompressible Euler equation without swirl, prove asymptotically self-similar blowup from low-regularity initial data, characterize the limiting behavior as α→(1/3)^-, and show sharpness of the result. This is the first example where blowup from a 1D model is lifted to construct 3D incompressible fluid singularity.
AI.motivation: To prove finite-time singularity formation in the 3D incompressible Euler equation (without swirl) via exact self-similar blowup profiles, addressing a major open problem in fluid dynamics and showing optimal regularity thresholds.
AI.method: The construction is inspired by the Hou–Zhang blowup scenario. A fixed-point argument lifts C^∞ blowup profiles from a 1D model (companion work [11]) to exact 3D profiles. Anisotropic weighted estimates and a novel integration-by-parts method along trajectories (exploiting the equation twice) overcome the lack of r-directional decay. A finite codimension stability argument in low-regularity settings proves asymptotic self-similar blowup.
AI.result: Existence of C^{1,α} self-similar blowup profiles for α∈(0,1/3); asymptotically self-similar blowup from C_c^α initial vorticity and C^{1,α}∩L^2 initial velocity; as α→(1/3)^-, the blowup rate diverges, and profiles factorize to a constant multiple of r^{1/3} times a C^∞ 1D profile. Sharpness is shown via global regularity theory for C^{1,1/3+}∩L^2 initial velocity.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first rigorous construction of 3D incompressible Euler blowup by lifting a 1D model singularity, with sharp regularity thresholds. This establishes the Hou–Zhang scenario as a valid blowup mechanism and opens new directions for singularity analysis in fluid equations.

title: Eradicating Negative Transfer in Multi-Physics Foundation Models via Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Routing
summary: Scaling Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) toward universal foundation models is bottlenecked by negative transfer: the simultaneous co-training of disparate partial differential equation (PDE) regimes can induce gradient conflict, unstable optimization, and plasticity loss in dense neural operators. In particular, broadband open-channel fluid dynamics and boundary-dominated porous media flows impose incompatible spectral and geometric demands on a single dense parameter path. We introduce Shodh-MoE, a sparse-activated latent transformer architecture for multi-physics transport. Shodh-MoE operates on compressed 16^3 physical latents produced by a physics-informed autoencoder with an intra-tokenizer Helmholtz-style velocity parameterization, restricting decoded states to divergence-free velocity manifolds. The model guarantees exact mass conservation, achieving a physically verifiable velocity divergence of ~2.8 x 10^-10 (evaluated post-hoc in FP64) on 128^3 grids. A Top-1 soft-semantic router dynamically assigns localized latent patches to expert subnetworks, enabling specialized parameter paths for distinct physical mechanisms while preserving shared experts for universal symmetries. In a 20,000-step distributed pretraining run over mixed three-dimensional physical tensors, routing telemetry shows autonomous domain bifurcation: held-out validation tokens from the open-channel domain route exclusively to Expert 0, while porous-media tokens route exclusively to Expert 1. The model converges simultaneously across both regimes, achieving latent validation MSEs of 2.46 x 10^-5 and 9.76 x 10^-6, and decoded physical MSEs of 2.48 x 10^-6 and 1.76 x 10^-6. These results support sparse expert routing as a practical architectural mechanism for mitigating multi-physics interference in universal neural operators.

AI.tldr: Shodh-MoE introduces a sparse-activated latent transformer with expert routing to overcome negative transfer when co-training neural operators across disparate PDE regimes (open-channel fluid dynamics vs. porous media flows), achieving physically verifiable mass conservation and autonomous domain bifurcation in training.
AI.motivation: Scaling SciML toward universal foundation models is hindered by negative transfer—co-training disparate PDE regimes causes gradient conflict, unstable optimization, and plasticity loss in dense neural operators.
AI.method: Shodh-MoE uses a sparse-activated latent transformer operating on compressed 16^3 latents from a physics-informed autoencoder with Helmholtz-style velocity parameterization (guaranteeing divergence-free fields), plus a Top-1 soft-semantic router assigning latent patches to specialized or shared expert subnetworks.
AI.result: Achieves verified divergence of ~2.8×10⁻¹⁰ on 128³ grids; autonomous domain bifurcation in routing (open-channel→Expert 0, porous-media→Expert 1); latent MSEs of 2.46×10⁻⁵ and 9.76×10⁻⁶, decoded physical MSEs of 2.48×10⁻⁶ and 1.76×10⁻⁶.
AI.conclusion: Sparse expert routing is a practical architectural mechanism for mitigating multi-physics interference in universal neural operators, enabling simultaneous convergence across disparate physical regimes.

title: Adaptive Metrics for Norm-Minimization-Based Outer Approximation in Convex Vector Optimization
summary: We develop an adaptive-metric framework for norm-minimization-based outer approximation algorithms in bounded convex vector optimization. The key idea is to let the scalarization metric vary across iterations while measuring approximation error in a fixed Euclidean norm. This enables the algorithm to exploit problem geometry dynamically. Our approach rests on two theoretical foundations. First, we prove that the improved Euclidean convergence rate $O(k^{2/(1-q)})$ -- previously known only for the standard $\ell_2$ norm -- extends to all fixed inner-product norms. Second, we establish a dispersion theorem showing that the cut normals generated by the algorithm naturally spread across all directions when the upper image has a strictly convex boundary with bounded curvature. This geometric condition guarantees that the adaptive metric remains well-conditioned throughout execution. Building on these results, we derive explicit convergence bounds that quantify how metric conditioning influences the Hausdorff error estimates. Numerical experiments validate the theoretical rates and demonstrate that adaptive metrics achieve 31--33\% fewer iterations than the fixed Euclidean norm on problems with curved Pareto fronts. Our results provide a rigorous foundation for adaptive metric selection in convex vector optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an adaptive-metric framework for outer approximation algorithms in convex vector optimization, proving convergence rates and demonstrating 31-33% iteration reductions over fixed Euclidean norms.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency of norm-minimization-based outer approximation algorithms by allowing the scalarization metric to adapt dynamically, exploiting problem geometry to reduce iterations.
AI.method: An adaptive-metric framework where scalarization metrics vary per iteration while measuring approximation error in a fixed Euclidean norm, supported by theoretical convergence proofs (Euclidean convergence rate O(k^{2/(1-q)}) for all fixed inner-product norms, dispersion theorem for cut normals) and numerical validation.
AI.result: Proved convergence rates extend to all fixed inner-product norms; established dispersion theorem for strictly convex boundaries; numerical experiments show 31-33% fewer iterations with adaptive metrics vs. fixed Euclidean norm on curved Pareto fronts.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive-metric framework provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for adaptive metric selection in convex vector optimization, significantly improving practical efficiency on problems with curved Pareto fronts.

title: Asymptotically Self-Similar Blowup for 3D Incompressible Euler with $C^{1, 1/3-}$ Velocity I: $C^{\infty}$ 1D Limiting Profiles
summary: We consider a one-parameter family of 1D models for the 3D axisymmetric incompressible Euler equation with $C^α$ vorticity and without swirl near the symmetry axis. For $α= \frac13$, we impose a crucial normalization and construct a $C^{\infty}$ self-similar blowup profile with unbounded 1D stream function and infinite spatial blowup rate, using a fixed-point argument around a numerically constructed approximate profile. For $α< \frac13$ sufficiently close to $\frac13$, we perturb the $\frac13$-profile and analytically construct exact smooth 1D profiles with bounded stream function and finite spatial blowup rate. In the companion work~\cite{chen2026eulerII}, for any $α\in (0,\frac13)$, we lift these 1D blowup profiles to construct exact $C^{1,α}$ self-similar blowup profiles for 3D Euler, and build on them to prove sharp asymptotically self-similar blowup for 3D axisymmetric Euler without swirl from $C_c^α$ initial vorticity and $C^{1,α} \cap L^2$ initial velocity.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs self-similar blowup profiles for 3D axisymmetric Euler equations without swirl, analyzing a one-parameter family of 1D models. For α=1/3, a C∞ blowup profile with unbounded stream function is constructed; for α<1/3 near 1/3, smooth profiles with bounded stream function are obtained via perturbation. These profiles enable exact C^{1,α} self-similar blowup solutions for the full 3D Euler system in a companion work.
AI.motivation: To understand singularity formation (blowup) in the 3D incompressible Euler equations, specifically for axisymmetric flows without swirl, by constructing exact self-similar blowup profiles parameterized by Hölder exponent α of the vorticity.
AI.method: The authors consider a 1D reduction of the 3D axisymmetric Euler equation near the symmetry axis. For α=1/3, they impose a normalization and use a fixed-point argument around a numerically computed approximate profile to construct a C∞ self-similar profile with unbounded stream function and infinite spatial blowup rate. For α<1/3 sufficiently close to 1/3, they perturb the α=1/3 profile to obtain smooth profiles with bounded stream function and finite blowup rate.
AI.result: Construction of C∞ self-similar blowup profiles for the 1D model: unbounded stream function case for α=1/3 and bounded case for α<1/3 near 1/3. These profiles serve as building blocks for lifting to exact C^{1,α} self-similar blowup profiles for the full 3D Euler system in a companion paper.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous analytical construction of self-similar blowup profiles for a 1D model relevant to 3D axisymmetric Euler blowup, bridging numerical approximations and exact analysis via fixed-point arguments and perturbation methods.

title: Convergence Rates for $\ell_p$ Norm Minimization in Convex Vector Optimization
summary: We analyze convergence rates of norm-minimization-based outer approximation algorithms for convex vector optimization when the scalarization uses an $\ell_p$ norm with $p \in (1,\infty)$. While the Euclidean case ($p=2$) achieves the optimal rate $O(k^{2/(1-q)})$, the behavior under general $\ell_p$ norms has remained open. A direct approach via the modulus of smoothness yields only the weaker exponent $\min(p,2)$, which degrades for $1 < p < 2$. We prove that the Hausdorff approximation error satisfies $δ_H(P_k, A) = O(k^{2/(1-q)})$ for \emph{every} $p \in (1,\infty)$, where $q$ is the number of objectives and $k$ is the iteration count. The proof introduces a Euclidean intermediary technique that exploits the ambient inner product structure of $\R^q$ to obtain a quadratic bound on the hyperplane distance, bypassing the $\ell_p$ smoothness limitation; norm equivalence then converts this to any $\ell_p$ metric at the cost of only a dimension-dependent constant, not a loss of exponent. Numerical experiments confirm the $p$-independent rate predicted by the theory.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that outer approximation algorithms for convex vector optimization achieve the optimal convergence rate O(k^{2/(1-q)}) for all ℓ_p norms (p∈(1,∞)), not just p=2, by introducing a Euclidean intermediary technique that bypasses limitations of direct modulus-of-smoothness analysis.
AI.motivation: The convergence rate of ℓ_p-norm-based outer approximation algorithms was only known for p=2 (optimal rate), while the behavior for general p remained open. Direct analysis via modulus of smoothness gives a weaker exponent min(p,2) that degrades when 1<p<2.
AI.method: Introduces a Euclidean intermediary technique: first derive a quadratic bound on hyperplane distance using the ambient inner product structure of ℝ^q, then convert to any ℓ_p metric via norm equivalence, incurring only a dimension-dependent constant rather than a loss of exponent.
AI.result: Proves that δ_H(P_k, A) = O(k^{2/(1-q)}) for every p ∈ (1,∞), where q is the number of objectives and k is the iteration count — matching the optimal rate previously known only for p=2.
AI.conclusion: The optimal convergence rate is p-independent, and numerical experiments confirm this theoretical prediction.

title: Boundary null-controllability for the beam equation with classical structural damping
summary:
Let $Δ$ be the Dirichlet Laplacian on the interval $(0,π)$, and let $T>0$. We prove a well-posedness results for the structurally damped beam equation $$u_{tt}+Δ^2 u-ρΔu_t=0, x\in (0,π),t>0$$ with various boundary conditions including $$ u(0,t)=u_{xx}(0,t)=0; u(π,t)=f(t),u_{xx}(π,t)=0, $$ and $f\in H_0^2(0,T)$ and appropriate initial conditions. Viewing $f$ as a control, we prove null controllability for all $ρ\leq 2$. For $ρ>2$, we show null controllability for arbitrary $T>0$ holds for almost all $ρ$, but fails for a dense subset of $(2,\infty)$.
  An analagous result is proven for Neumann control.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the well-posedness and null controllability of a structurally damped beam equation with Dirichlet and Neumann boundary controls, showing that null controllability holds for all ρ ≤ 2, but for ρ > 2 it holds for almost all ρ yet fails for a dense subset of (2,∞).
AI.motivation: To understand how structural damping affects the controllability of beam equations, particularly the transition in behavior at the damping threshold ρ = 2.
AI.method: The authors analyze the Dirichlet Laplacian on (0,π) and study the well-posedness of the beam equation with various boundary conditions, including non-homogeneous Dirichlet/Neumann controls. They use functional analytic and spectral methods to prove controllability properties.
AI.result: Null controllability holds for all ρ ≤ 2. For ρ > 2, null controllability holds for almost all ρ (any T>0) but fails for a dense subset of (2,∞). Analogous results hold for Neumann boundary control.
AI.conclusion: The damping parameter ρ critically determines controllability: below/at threshold 2, control is always possible; above threshold 2, controllability exhibits a pathological dense/fractal behavior depending on specific ρ values.

title: The radial Newton problem: nonlinear dynamics of minimal resistance in central fields
summary: This paper investigates the nonlinear dynamics of Newton's problem of minimal resistance in radial fields. We move beyond classical translational symmetry to analyze two non-equilibrium scenarios: a scale-invariant free expansion and an incompressible source flow. Our analysis reveals that the scale-invariant model suffers from a symmetry-breaking instability (loss of ellipticity) that necessitates geometric truncation. Conversely, we prove that the incompressible flow acts as a structural regularizer, admitting unique, smooth, and strictly concave solutions. These findings provide new qualitative insights into how physical conservation laws ensure the regularity and symmetry of optimal configurations in high-speed central flows, bridging the gap between variational calculus and the physics of complex systems.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes nonlinear dynamics in Newton's minimal resistance problem within radial fields, showing that scale-invariant free expansion leads to symmetry-breaking instability requiring geometric truncation, while incompressible source flow acts as a structural regularizer producing unique, smooth, concave solutions.
AI.motivation: To understand how physical conservation laws affect the regularity and symmetry of optimal configurations in high-speed central flows, bridging variational calculus and complex systems physics.
AI.method: The authors move beyond classical translational symmetry to analyze two non-equilibrium scenarios: a scale-invariant free expansion and an incompressible source flow, using nonlinear dynamics analysis and variational methods.
AI.result: The scale-invariant model exhibits a symmetry-breaking instability (loss of ellipticity) requiring geometric truncation, while the incompressible flow model proves to admit unique, smooth, and strictly concave solutions.
AI.conclusion: Physical conservation laws (specifically incompressibility) can ensure regularity and symmetry in optimal configurations, providing new insights into the intersection of variational calculus and complex systems.

title: DiffPhD: A Unified Differentiable Solver for Projective Heterogeneous Materials in Elastodynamics with Contact-Rich GPU-Acceleration
summary: Differentiable simulation of soft bodies is a foundation for system identification, trajectory optimization, and Real2Sim transfer. Yet, existing methods such as the differentiable Projective Dynamics (DiffPD) struggle when faced with heterogeneous materials with extreme stiffness contrasts, hyperelasticity under large deformations, and contact-rich interactions, which are common scenarios in the real world. We present DiffPhD, a unified GPU-accelerated differentiable Projective Dynamics framework for heterogeneous materials that tackles these intertwined challenges simultaneously. Our key insight is a careful integration of: (i) stiffness-aware projective weights to embed heterogeneity into the global system; (ii) trust-region eigenvalue filtering lifted to the backward pass for stable hyperelastic gradients and a type-II Anderson Acceleration scheme with dual-gate convergence to stabilize forward iteration under large stiffness contrasts; and (iii) a unified GPU pipeline that reuses a single sparse factor across forward, backward, and contact computations, with stiffness-amplified Rayleigh damping folded into the same factor for heterogeneity-aware dissipation at zero recurring cost. DiffPhD achieves strict gradient accuracy while delivering up to an order-of-magnitude speedup over prior differentiable solvers on heterogeneous, hyperelastic, contact-rich benchmarks. Crucially, this speedup does not come at the cost of stability: DiffPhD remains convergent on stiffness contrasts up to 100x where prior PD solvers degrade. This unlocks end-to-end gradient-based optimization on regimes previously bottlenecked by either solver fragility or per-iteration cost -- shell--joint composite creatures, soft characters wielding stiff weapons, and soft-gripper robotic manipulation -- all handled within a single forward--backward pass.

AI.tldr: DiffPhD is a GPU-accelerated differentiable Projective Dynamics framework that enables stable, accurate simulation of heterogeneous soft bodies with extreme stiffness contrasts, hyperelasticity, and contact-rich interactions, achieving up to 10x speedup over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing differentiable soft-body simulators (e.g., DiffPD) struggle with heterogeneous materials, extreme stiffness contrasts, hyperelasticity under large deformations, and contact-rich interactions — real-world scenarios — due to solver fragility and high per-iteration costs.
AI.method: Three key integrations: (i) stiffness-aware projective weights for material heterogeneity; (ii) trust-region eigenvalue filtering for stable backward gradients plus type-II Anderson Acceleration with dual-gate convergence for robust forward iteration; (iii) a unified GPU pipeline reusing a single sparse factor across forward, backward, and contact passes, with stiffness-amplified Rayleigh damping folded in at zero extra cost.
AI.result: Up to 10x speedup over prior differentiable solvers on heterogeneous, hyperelastic, contact-rich benchmarks. Converges on stiffness contrasts up to 100x where prior PD solvers degrade. Strict gradient accuracy maintained.
AI.conclusion: DiffPhD unlocks end-to-end gradient-based optimization for previously intractable regimes (shell-joint composites, soft characters with stiff weapons, soft-gripper manipulation) in a single forward-backward pass, without sacrificing stability or speed.

title: A tree-free approach to 3D Yang-Mills Langevin dynamic. Analytic estimates and the existence of a model for a regularity structure
summary: Using the multi-index approach to regularity structures due to F. Otto et al., we construct a regularity structure and a model for it associated to the stochastic Langevin equation for the 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills functional. For the model we also obtain global stochastic and global pointwise weighted Besov type estimates which hold almost surely. The model is defined as a limit of a sequence of smooth models introduced with the help of a mollified noise. When the mollification is removed the sequence converges in a certain topology defined with the help of the stochastic estimates. To obtain these results we develop the multi-index approach for systems of equations with vector-valued white noises. This project is motivated by the problem for constructing 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills measure and by the earlier results of the author on the related problem of canonical quantization of the Yang-Mills field on the Minkowski space.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a regularity structure and model for the stochastic Langevin equation of 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills using the multi-index approach, proving convergence of smooth models with mollified noise and obtaining global stochastic and pointwise weighted Besov estimates.
AI.motivation: To construct the 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills measure and build upon earlier results on canonical quantization of Yang-Mills fields on Minkowski space.
AI.method: Uses the multi-index approach to regularity structures (F. Otto et al.) to construct a regularity structure and model for the stochastic Langevin equation of 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills. Employs mollified noise to define smooth models, then proves convergence in a specific topology using stochastic estimates. Extends the multi-index approach to systems with vector-valued white noises.
AI.result: Construction of a regularity structure and model for the 3D Yang-Mills stochastic Langevin equation, with global stochastic and pointwise weighted Besov estimates holding almost surely. The smooth models converge when mollification is removed.
AI.conclusion: The developed framework provides a foundation for constructing the 3D Euclidean Yang-Mills measure and extends the multi-index regularity structures approach to vector-valued noise systems.

title: Unbiased and Second-Order-Free Training for High-Dimensional PDEs
summary: Deep learning methods based on backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) have emerged as competitive alternatives to physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs). By leveraging probabilistic representations, BSDE approaches can avoid the curse of dimensionality and often admit second-order-free training objectives that do not require explicit Hessian evaluations. It has recently been established that the commonly used Euler-Maruyama (EM) time discretization induces an intrinsic bias in BSDE training losses. While high-order schemes such as Heun can fully eliminate this bias, such schemes re-introduce second-order spatial derivatives and incur substantial computational overhead. In this work, we provide a principled analysis of EM-induced loss bias and propose an unbiased, second-order-free training framework that preserves the computational advantages of BSDE methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/seojaemin22/Un-EM-BSDE.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies and analyzes bias from Euler-Maruyama discretization in BSDE-based PDE solvers, then proposes an unbiased, second-order-free training framework that maintains computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: BSDE methods for high-dimensional PDEs suffer from an intrinsic bias induced by Euler-Maruyama time discretization, while high-order schemes like Heun eliminate bias but reintroduce costly second-order derivatives.
AI.method: The paper provides a principled analysis of the EM-induced loss bias and proposes a novel unbiased training framework that avoids second-order spatial derivatives, preserving computational advantages of BSDE methods.
AI.result: The proposed framework eliminates EM-induced bias without requiring explicit Hessian evaluations, maintaining efficiency comparable to standard BSDE approaches.
AI.conclusion: An unbiased, second-order-free BSDE training method is achieved, offering a practical and efficient solution for high-dimensional PDE solving without the bias-compromise trade-off.

title: Fredholm Criteria for $G$-pseudodifferential Operators
summary: Let $G$ be a compact Lie group that acts smoothly on a closed manifold $M$. Using a general Simonenko principle, we derive a novel criterion for the Fredholm property of $G$-pseudodifferential operators acting on Sobolev spaces of sections of vector bundles over $M$. In case the group is finite, we obtain a further characterization of the Fredholm property of $G$-pseudodifferential operators in terms of the invertibility of suitable symbols.

AI.tldr: The paper develops Fredholm criteria for G-pseudodifferential operators on compact manifolds with Lie group actions, using a Simonenko principle, and provides a symbolic characterization for finite groups.
AI.motivation: To establish necessary and sufficient conditions for G-pseudodifferential operators to be Fredholm on manifolds equipped with smooth Lie group actions, extending existing Fredholm theory to equivariant settings.
AI.method: The authors employ a Simonenko principle adapted to the G-equivariant context to derive a Fredholm criterion for G-pseudodifferential operators acting on Sobolev spaces. For finite groups, they further refine this to a condition based on invertibility of suitable symbols.
AI.result: A novel criterion for the Fredholm property of G-pseudodifferential operators is obtained via the Simonenko principle. For finite group actions, a more explicit characterization in terms of symbol invertibility is established.
AI.conclusion: The work provides new tools for analyzing Fredholmness in equivariant pseudodifferential theory, with a clean symbolic characterization when the acting group is finite.

title: T-E formulation-based modeling of thin HTS shell magnetization
summary:
Numerical methods for modeling thin-film magnetization are primarily focused on computing the current density distribution. The highly nonlinear current-voltage characteristic of type-II superconductors significantly complicates the accurate computation of the electric field. The T-E formulation-based mixed finite element method, previously derived for flat superconducting films, enables the simultaneous, accurate determination of both variables. Another advantage of this method is that the computational domain is limited to the film itself: no meshing of the surrounding space is required. The thin-shell approximation reduces the problem to a two-dimensional one.
  This work extends the T-E formulation and numerical method to non-flat superconducting shells with a metal substrate. We validate the method with several test examples, including modeling the magnetization of a sphere. The method is then applied to a realistic model of a cylindrical magnetic dynamo pump, and the generated open-circuit voltage is computed.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the T-E formulation mixed finite element method for modeling thin-film magnetization from flat films to non-flat superconducting shells with metal substrates, validated through test examples including a sphere and applied to a cylindrical magnetic dynamo pump to compute open-circuit voltage.
AI.motivation: To extend existing T-E formulation based mixed finite element methods from flat superconducting films to non-flat superconducting shells with metal substrates, enabling accurate modeling of realistic curved geometries.
AI.method: The authors extend the T-E formulation (electric vector potential T and electric field E) and mixed finite element method to non-flat superconducting shells. The thin-shell approximation reduces the 3D problem to 2D, and the computational domain is limited to the film/shell itself without meshing the surrounding space.
AI.result: The method is validated with test examples including modeling the magnetization of a sphere, and successfully applied to a realistic cylindrical magnetic dynamo pump model to compute the generated open-circuit voltage.
AI.conclusion: The T-E formulation method can be effectively extended to non-flat superconducting geometries with metal substrates, providing accurate simultaneous computation of current density and electric field for realistic engineering applications.

title: FKPP fronts in quenched random media
summary: We study numerically the evolution of one-dimensional FKPP fronts initiated from steep initial conditions in the presence of a quenched random growth rate. Compared to both the homogeneous case (with velocity $v_0$) and deterministic disorder, quenched randomness increases the average propagation speed. We show that the velocity shift relative to the homogeneous case scales linearly with the disorder variance $σ^2$, with a universal prefactor -- independent of the specific distribution of the disorder -- such that $v = v_0 + a σ^2$, with $a \approx 0.02432 \pm 0.00002$. Moreover, the front position exhibits diffusive fluctuations across disorder realizations. The corresponding effective diffusion coefficient scales quadratically with $σ$, $D = \frac{b^2 σ^2}{2}$, with $b \approx 0.223 \pm 0.002$. These results suggest a universal statistical response of FKPP fronts to quenched heterogeneity.

AI.tldr: This paper numerically studies FKPP fronts with quenched random growth rates, finding that disorder increases propagation speed linearly with variance (universal prefactor ~0.024) and induces diffusive front fluctuations with diffusion coefficient scaling quadratically with disorder strength.
AI.motivation: To understand how quenched (static) randomness in growth rates affects the propagation of FKPP fronts, which has applications in population dynamics, combustion, and reaction-diffusion systems in heterogeneous environments.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of one-dimensional FKPP equations with steep initial conditions and quenched random growth rate disorder, measuring front velocity and position fluctuations across disorder realizations.
AI.result: Front velocity shift scales linearly with disorder variance σ² (v = v₀ + aσ², a≈0.02432), and front position fluctuations exhibit diffusive behavior with diffusion coefficient D = b²σ²/2 (b≈0.223).
AI.conclusion: FKPP fronts respond universally to quenched heterogeneity, with disorder increasing propagation speed and inducing diffusive fluctuations—findings independent of the specific disorder distribution.

title: Minimal submanifolds confined in space
summary: Already in $\bf{R}^4$, there are many known examples of minimal hypersurfaces, yet few structural results. We show that minimal submanifolds, of any dimension, that are confined in space are very restricted. It is well-known that the half-space theorem fails already for hypersurfaces in $\bf{R}^4$, where there are many examples contained in a slab. In $\bf{R}^3$ the height of the catenoid grows at a logarithmic rate, whereas in higher dimension the height of the catenoid remains bounded. We will see that even in high dimensions, minimal submanifolds that are confined in space must satisfy strong structural restrictions. We show that any proper minimal immersion whose height grows sublinearly must have Euclidean volume growth. A consequence is an optimal Bernstein theorem in any dimension for stable hypersurfaces with sublinearly growing height that generalizes results of Moser, Bombieri-De Giorgi-Miranda, Trudinger, Caffarelli-Nirenberg-Spruck and Ecker-Huisken.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that minimal submanifolds confined in space must satisfy strong structural restrictions, including that proper minimal immersions with sublinear height growth must have Euclidean volume growth, leading to an optimal Bernstein theorem for stable hypersurfaces in any dimension.
AI.motivation: In R^4, there are many examples of minimal hypersurfaces but few structural results. The half-space theorem fails in R^4 (unlike R^3), and higher-dimensional catenoids have bounded height unlike the logarithmic growth in R^3. The authors aim to find structural constraints for confined minimal submanifolds in high dimensions.
AI.method: The authors analyze proper minimal immersions with sublinear height growth and show they must have Euclidean volume growth. This is used to derive a Bernstein-type theorem for stable hypersurfaces.
AI.result: Any proper minimal immersion with sublinear height growth has Euclidean volume growth. This yields an optimal Bernstein theorem in any dimension for stable hypersurfaces with sublinearly growing height, generalizing results by Moser, Bombieri-De Giorgi-Miranda, Trudinger, Caffarelli-Nirenberg-Spruck, and Ecker-Huisken.
AI.conclusion: Even in high dimensions where the half-space theorem fails and catenoids have bounded height, confined minimal submanifolds still satisfy strong structural restrictions.

title: Liouville theorem for immersed minimal surfaces in any codimension
summary: For a proper immersed minimal disk in $\bf{R}^N$ with quadratic area growth, we show that any harmonic function whose negative part grows at a slow sub-linear rate is constant. This leads to a higher codimensional Bernstein theorem for minimal disks contained in a sub-linearly growing cone. The catenoid, helicoid and Enneper's family of surfaces together show that this result is optimal. We also show uniform Hölder regularity of harmonic functions.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that harmonic functions with sub-linearly growing negative part are constant on immersed minimal disks with quadratic area growth, leading to a higher codimensional Bernstein theorem for minimal disks in sub-linearly growing cones. The result is shown optimal via catenoid, helicoid, and Enneper's surfaces, and uniform Hölder regularity is also established.
AI.motivation: To extend Bernstein-type theorems to higher codimensions and establish optimal conditions under which minimal disks must be linear, addressing the gap between known results for minimal surfaces in higher dimensions.
AI.method: The proof uses analysis of harmonic functions on immersed minimal disks with quadratic area growth, combining techniques from geometric measure theory and partial differential equations, along with Hölder regularity estimates.
AI.result: 1) Any harmonic function with sub-linearly growing negative part is constant on such minimal disks. 2) A higher codimensional Bernstein theorem for minimal disks in sub-linearly growing cones. 3) The result is sharp, as shown by catenoid, helicoid, and Enneper surfaces. 4) Uniform Hölder regularity holds for harmonic functions.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides an optimal Bernstein-type theorem in higher codimensions, showing that minimal disks in slowly growing cones must be linear, with examples demonstrating sharpness.

title: Noether symmetries and conservation laws of a class of time-dependent multidimensional nonlinear wave equations
summary: Conservation laws of a class of time-dependent damped nonlinear multidimensional wave equations are derived by Noether's theorem. For arbitrary nonzero damping coefficient and nonlinear interaction term, its infinitesimal variational symmetries span a Euclidean algebra $\euclid(n)$ of space translations and rotations. They produce conservation of linear and angular momentums. For some specific forms of these two terms symmetry algebra is enlarged to a subalgebra of the conformal algebra $\conf(1,n)$ and in this case more interesting conservation laws are found.

AI.tldr: This paper derives conservation laws for time-dependent damped nonlinear wave equations using Noether's theorem. The symmetries of the equations span Euclidean algebra (space translations and rotations) for arbitrary nonzero damping, producing conservation of linear and angular momentum. For specific forms of the damping and nonlinear terms, the symmetry algebra enlarges to a subalgebra of the conformal algebra, revealing additional conservation laws.
AI.motivation: To systematically derive conservation laws for damped nonlinear wave equations using symmetry principles, since conservation laws are fundamental for understanding physical systems and their dynamics.
AI.method: Noether's theorem is applied to identify infinitesimal variational symmetries of the damped nonlinear wave equations. The Euclidean algebra is found for arbitrary damping, and the symmetry algebra is shown to enlarge to a conformal subalgebra for specific forms of the damping coefficient and nonlinear interaction term.
AI.result: For arbitrary nonzero damping, symmetries span the Euclidean algebra (space translations and rotations), yielding conservation of linear and angular momentum. For specific damping and nonlinear terms, the symmetry algebra extends to a conformal subalgebra, producing additional conservation laws.
AI.conclusion: The conservation laws of damped nonlinear wave equations depend crucially on the specific forms of damping and nonlinear terms. A richer set of conservation laws emerges when the symmetry algebra expands to include conformal symmetries.
